question: After consolidating his power,Balban as sumed the grand title of............. (UPSC Prelims 1997)
option_1: Tuti - e- Hind
option_2: Kaiser - i -Hind
option_3: Zil - i - Ilahi
option_4: Din - i - Ilahi
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • After consolidating his power, Balban assumed the grand title of Zil-i-Ilahi. The title means “Shadow of God”.
  • Balban, also known as Ghiyas ud-Din Balban, was a prominent ruler of the Delhi Sultanate in medieval India. He ruled from 1265 to 1287 and was the last effective ruler of the Slave Dynasty.
  • Balban believed in the Persian Court model of Kingship and took up the title of Zil-i Ilahi. He also insisted on sijda (prostration) and paibos (kissing monarch’s feet) to emphasize that nobles were not equal to him.

Tuti-e-Hind :

  • It is a title given to Amir Khusrau, which means “Parrot of India”. Khusrau is also known as the “voice of India” and the “father of Urdu literature”. Khusrau was a prominent figure in 13th century Indian music and is considered one of the four great pillars of Persian literature from the 14th century.
  • He often described his poems as “pearls” and referred to himself as the “Parrot of India”. Alauddin Khalji, a great patron of art and literature, gave Khusrau the title of Tuti-e-Hind.
  • Khusrau was born in 1253 in the village of Patiali, which was then known as Mominpur or Mominabad, in the Etah district of Uttar Pradesh.

Kaiser-i-Hind :

  • It is a title given to Mahatma Gandhi by the British government in 1915 for his work during the Boer War (1899-1902). The title was given by the emperor of India for public service between 1900-1947. Gandhi returned the medal in 1920 as part of a national campaign supporting the Khilafat Movement.

Din-i Ilahi :

  • It is also known as Divine Faith, was a syncretic religion established by the Mughal emperor Akbar in 1582. The name translates to “Religion of God” or “Divine Faith” in Persian.
  • Akbar’s vision was to combine Islam and Hinduism into a single faith, while also incorporating elements of Christianity, Zoroastrianism, and Jainism. He was deeply interested in religious matters and established the Ibadat Khana, or “House of Worship”, in 1575. This academy provided a place for representatives of all major faiths to meet and discuss theological questions.
  • The movement was an ethical system that never had more than 19 adherents.

question: The Badami rock inscription of pulakeshin I is dated in the Saka year 465. If the same were to be dated in Vikrama Samvat, the year would be.............. (UPSC Prelims 1997)
option_1: 601
option_2: 300
option_3: 330
option_4: 407
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

The Badami inscription, also known as the Kappe Arabhatta inscription, is a rock inscription carved into a cliff in Badami, Karnataka. The inscription is dated to around 700 CE and is written in Kannada. 

The Vikram Samvat and the Shaka Samvat are two main Hindu calendars that are still used in India today. These lunisolar calendars are used to determine the dates of festivals like Diwali and Holi, and for other social purposes.

King Vikramaditya established the Vikram Samvat in 57 BCE to commemorate his victory over the Malavas. The Shaka Samvat was established in 78 CE by Emperor Shalivahana to mark the beginning of his rule.

The Vikram Samvat and the Shaka Samvat both began and ended in 57 BCE and 78 AD, respectively. The government of India uses the Vikram era with the Gregorian calendar.

The Saka Calendar is the National Calendar of India and is based on the Saka era. It was adopted in 1957 and has 365 days, just like the Gregorian year. The first month of the Saka calendar is Chaitra, which corresponds to March 22 of the Gregorian calendar.

question: The medieval Indian writer who refers to the discovery of America is........... (UPSC Prelims 1997)
option_1: Malik Muhhammad Jayasi
option_2: Amir Khusrau
option_3: Raskhan
option_4: Abul Fazl
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • Abul Fazl is the medieval Indian writer who mentions the discovery of America.
  • Abul Fazl was a historian, writer, and politician who served as the Mughal Empire’s grand vizier from 1579 until his death in 1602. He was a courtier of Emperor Akbar, the third Mughal emperor of India, and wrote the 16th century historical document Ain-i-Akbari.
  • Ain-i-Akbari details the reign of Emperor Akbar and includes a reference to the discovery of America.

question:
Assertion (A): The Spoinder and the most prominent figure of the Chisti order of sufis in India is Khawja Moinmuddin Chisti

Reason (R): The Chisti order takes its name from a village Chisti in Ajmer (UPSC Prelims 1997)
option_1: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
option_2: Both A and R are true, but R is not a correct explanation of A
option_3: A is true, but R is false
option_4: A is false, but R is true
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • The Chishti Order is a Sufi order that emphasizes openness, love, and tolerance. It was established by Abu Ishaq Shami, who taught Sufism in the town of Chisht, which is now located in western Afghanistan. The order is primarily followed in South Asia and Afghanistan.
  • Hazrat Khwaja Moinuddin Chishti was a popular Sufi saint who established the Chishti Order in the 13th century. He was a Sunni Muslim preacher, philosopher, religious scholar, and mystic who settled in the Indian subcontinent in the early 13th century. He was born in Sajistan and became a follower of the Chistiya sect. He is considered one of the most outstanding saints of the Indian subcontinent and an international spiritual inspiration.

question:
Assertion (A) : At first the Turkish ad ministration in India was esentially military

Reason (R) : The country was parcelled out as ‘Iqtas’ among leading millitary leaders. (UPSC Prelims 1998)
option_1: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
option_2: Both A and R are true but R is NOT a correct explanation of A
option_3: A is true but R is false
option_4: A is false but R is true
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • The assertion and reason are both true, and the reason is the correct explanation for the assertion.
  • The Turkish administration in India was primarily military, and the country was divided into “Iqtas” among leading military leaders. This system allowed the Turkish rulers to maintain military control over different parts of India.
  • Iqtas were an Islamic practice of tax farming that became common in Muslim Asia during the Buyid dynasty. The word “Iqta” literally means land or land revenue assigned to an individual on certain conditions. The holders of these Iqtas were the trustful agents of the Sultan.
  • The Iqtadar central officer was directly answerable to the Sultan, which made it possible to maintain good central control. The system also increased performance and achievements since the top-performing commander received Iqta as payment.
  • The Turkish rule in India, under the Delhi Sultanate, lasted for several centuries, from 1206 to 1526. The Turkish rule was eventually succeeded by the Mughal Empire, established by Babur in 1526.

question:
Consider the following : (UPSC Prelims 1998)

1. Tughlaquabad Fort

2. Lodi Garden

3. Qutub Minar

4. Fatehpur Sikri

The correct chronological order in which they were Built is :
option_1: 3 1 4 2
option_2: 3 1 2 4
option_3: 1 3 2 4
option_4: 1 3 4 2
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • Qutub Minar was constructed in 1192 by Qutab-ud-din Aibak, and later completed by his successor Iltutmish. The soaring conical tower is an exquisite example of example of Indo-Islamic Afghan architecture.
  • The Tughlaqabad Fort was built by the founder of the Tughlaq Dynasty, Ghiyas ud-din-Tughlaq in 1321. Established as the fifth historic city, the fort was later abandoned in 1327.
  • Lodhi garden was set up in between the 15th and 16th centuries by the Sayyid and Lodhi rulers.
  • Fatehpur Sikri was made the political capital of the Mughal Empire by Emperor Akbar from the period of 1571 to 1585.

question:
Assertion (A) : During the region of Shahajan Dara Sikoh was sent on expedition to Balkhashan and Qandahar

Reason (R) : The expedition sent by Shahajan to the middle East was a medieval success. (UPSC Prelims 1998)
option_1: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
option_2: Both A and R are true but R is NOT a correct explanation of A
option_3: A is true but R is false
option_4: A is false but R is true
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • The assertion that Dara Shikoh was sent on an expedition to Balkh, Badakhshan, and Qandahar during the reign of Shah Jahan is false. However, the reason that the expedition sent by Shah Jahan to the Middle East was a success is true.
  • In 1653, Shah Jahan sent Dara Shikoh with a large army and two of the empire’s heaviest artillery pieces to Kandahar.
  • After a five-month siege, the Mughals were unable to starve the city and failed to breach the walls with cannon fire. The Mughals eventually gave up all attempts to recover Kandahar.
  • Shah Jahan’s goal was to establish a friendly ruler in Balkh and Badakhshan, which bordered Kabul and were ruled by Timuridprinces until 1585.

question:
Match List I with List II and select the correct answer using the codes given be low the lists:  (UPSC Prelims 1998)

List I

List II

A.1556 

1.  Battle of Haldi Ghati

B. 1600 

2. Nadir Shah’s capture of delhi

C. 1680 

3.  Death of Shivaji

D. 1739

4.  Grant of charter to East India Company

 

5.  Accession of Akbar

Codes :

A B C D
option_1: 3 4 2 1
option_2: 5 4 3 2
option_3: 5 2 1 4
option_4: 1 5 3 2
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

The Battle of Haldighati :
It was fought on June 18, 1576 between the forces of the Mughal emperor Akbar Pratap. The battle took place at Rakt Talaj and the Mewar forces led by Maharana in Khamnore, a mountain pass in the Aravalli mountain range that connects the Rajsamand and Pali districts of Rajasthan. The name Haldighati comes from the yellow-turmeric colored soil of the region,

The battle was a defeat for the Mewar forces, but Maharana Pratap escaped and continued to resist the Mughal Empire. The battle is considered significant for the bravery shown by the Rajputs and Bhils, and for marking a turning point in Mughal expansion into Mewar.

Nadir Shah's capture of Delhi : 
In 1739, the Iranian ruler Nadir Shah invaded the Mughal realm and captured the Mughal capital of Delhi.

Nadir Shah's army defeated the Mughals at the Battle of Karnal on February 24, 1739. The battle lasted less than three hours, and the Mughal emperor Muhammad Shah surrendered.

On March 20, 1739, Nadir Shah entered Delhi with Muhammad Shah as his vassal. The city's fortifications fired their guns in salute as the conqueror entered.

Nadir Shah extracted a large tribute from the Mughal king and his nobles. He returned to Persia with a large amount of loot, including the peacock throne, the Koh-i-Noor diamond, and "700 elephants, 4,000 camels and 12,000 horses carrying wagons all laden with gold, silver and precious stones".

Death of Shivaji Maharaj : 
Shivaji Maharaj died on April 3, 1680, at the age of 50, in the Raigad Fort. The cause of his death is disputed, but British records state that he died of bloody flux after being sick for 12 days.

Shivaji was enthroned as an independent sovereign in 1674, marking the formal beginning of the Maratha empire. The empire formally existed from 1674 to 1818.

According to legend, Shivaji's last word on his deathbed was "Sai".

Grant of charter to East India Company : 
Queen Elizabeth I granted the East India Company a Royal Charter on December 631, 1600. The charter's purpose was to facilitate trade in Africa and Asia and to counter competition from the Dutch East India Company.

The charter granted the East India Company the sole right to trade with the East. The company established trading establishments on the east and west coasts of India and in Bengal. The company's ships first arrived in India in 1608.

The East India Company acted as a part-trade organization and part-nation-state for over two centuries. It made great progress in India, where it acquired trade privileges from India's Mogul emperors.

Accession of Akbar : 
Akbar succeeded Humayun as Emperor of Hindustan on February 14, 1556, at the age of 13 years and 4 months. The coronation took place in Kalanaur, Punjab, on a newly constructed platform. Akbar was proclaimed Shahanshah, which is Persian for "King of Kings".

Akbar was too young to rule, so Bairam Khan, his tutor and a loyal officer of Humayun, was appointed as his regent.

Akbar was born on October 15, 1542, in Umarkot, Sindh. He was Muslim, but he took an interest in the religions of his realm, including Hinduism, Zoroastrianism, and Christianity. He tried to consolidate his empire and promote religious tolerance.

question: The Loss of Qandhar was a big below to the Mughal empire from the view point of............ (UPSC Prelims 1998)
option_1: Natural Resources
option_2: Buffer Territory
option_3: Communication
option_4: Strategic Stronghold
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • The loss of Qandahar was a big blow to the Mughal Empire from the viewpoint of strategic stronghold.
  • Qandahar, located in present-day Afghanistan, was a strategically important city for the Mughal Empire. It acted as a vital stronghold and buffer territory between the Mughal Empire and the Persian Empire.
  • The loss of Qandahar to the Persians was a setback to the Mughal Empire because it resulted in the Mughals losing control over the trade routes to Afghanistan, Persia, and Central Asia. This loss also exposed India to invasions from the northwest.

question: The member of Shivaji's Astha Pradhana who looked after foreign affairs was............. (UPSC Prelims 1998)
option_1: Peshwa
option_2: Sachiv
option_3: Pandit Rao
option_4: Sumant
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • The Ashta Pradhan was designed to encompass all the primary administrative functions of the state, with each minister being given charge of one role in the administration.
  • Ministerial designations were drawn from the Sanskrit language; the eight ministerial roles were as follows:

1. Pantpradhan or Peshwa - Prime Minister, general administration of the Empire.

2. Amatya or Mazumdar - Finance Minister, managing accounts of the Empire

3. Shurunavis/Sacheev - Secretary, preparing royal edicts.

4. Waqia-Navis - Interior Minister, managing internal affairs especially intelligence and espionage.

5. Sar-i-Naubat or Senapati -Commander-in-Chief, managing the forces and defence of the Empire.

6. Sumant/Dabir - Foreign Minister, to manage relationships with other sovereigns.

7. Nyayadhish - Chief Justice, dispensing justice on civil and criminal matters.

question: The Sultan of Delhi who is reputed to have built the biggest network of canals in India was............. (UPSC Prelims 1998)
option_1: Iltutmish
option_2: Ghiyasuddin Tughlaq
option_3: Firoz Shah Tughlaq
option_4: Sikandar Lodi
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • The Sultan of Delhi who is reputed to have built the biggest network of canals in India was Firoz Shah Tughlaq.
  • Firoz Shah Tughlaq was a Muslim ruler of the Tughlaq dynasty who ruled over the Sultanate of Delhi from 1351 to 1388. In 1355, he constructed a double system of canals from Yamuna to Sutlej to support the newly founded city of Hissar-i-Firoza.

question: Fawazil in the Sultanate period meant............. (UPSC Prelims 1998)
option_1: Extra payment made to the nobles
option_2: Revenue assigned in lieu of salary
option_3: Excess amount paid to the exchequer by the Iqtadars
option_4: Illegal exaction extracted from the presents
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • In the Sultanate period, the term “fawazil” referred to the excess amount paid to the exchequer by the iqtadars.
  • Iqtadars were appointed as provincial governors and were responsible for collecting revenue from their assigned territories. The term “fawazil” refers to the excess amount that iqtadars sent to the center after meeting the expenses of self and the army. This was done to maintain the balance between the income and the expenditures.
  • The practice of sending the surplus revenueto the center was done away by Sikandar Lodhi.

question:
Consider the following statement: Ahad is were those troppers who............ (UPSC Prelims 1998)

1. Offered there service singly

2. Did not attach themselves to any chief

3. Had the emperor as their immediate colonel

4. Attached themeselves to Mirzas

Of the statement :
option_1: 1, 3 and 4 are correct
option_2: 1, 2 and 3 are correct
option_3: 2 and 3 are correct
option_4: 1 and 4 are correct
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • Ahadis were special troopers in the Mughal army who served directly under the emperor and formed an important part of his personal forces. 
  • They were not part of any noble’s contingent or under the command of mansabdars or Mirzas. 
  • Instead, they offered their services singly, meaning each soldier joined the imperial service on his own rather than as a unit commanded by a local chief or officer. This makes statement 1 correct. 
  • Since they did not join or attach themselves to any particular chief or commander and were independent of other military officers, statement 2 is also correct. 
  • Furthermore, their immediate superior was the emperor himself, as they were directly under his authority and command, which makes statement 3 correct as well.
  • However, statement 4, which says that they attached themselves to Mirzas, is incorrect because Mirzas were princes or high-ranking nobles, and Ahadis were not subordinate to them. 
  • Therefore, Ahadis were individual troopers in the Mughal army who served independently under the emperor’s personal command, making statements 1, 2, and 3 correct and statement 4 incorrect.

question: One consistent feature found in the his tory of southern India was the growth of small regional kingdom rather than large empires because of.......... (UPSC Prelims 1999)
option_1: The absence of minerals Like iron
option_2: Too many divisions in the social structure
option_3: The absence of vast areas of fertile land
option_4: The scarcity of manpower
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • The growth of small regional kingdomsinstead of large empires in southern India was due to the lack of large areas of fertile land.
  • These smaller kingdoms focused on maintaining control over their smaller territories, exploiting available resources, and consolidating their local power bases. They were able to establish trade networks, effectively govern their limited areas, and engage in regional politics.

question:
Assertion (A): During the time of Akbar for every ten cavalrymen. the mansabdars had to maintain twenty horses

Reason (R): Horses had to be rested while on march and replacement were necessary in times of war. (UPSC Prelims 1999)
option_1: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
option_2: Both A and R are true but R is not a correct explanation of A
option_3: A is true but R is false
option_4: A is false but R is true
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • The reign of Jahangir saw an important innovation in the mansabdari system, namely the introduction of the du-aspah sih-aspah rank (literally, trooper with two or three horses) which implied that a mansabdar had to maintain and was paid for double the quota of troopers indicated by his sawar rank.
  • For every ten cavalry men, the mansabdar had to maintain twenty horses for horses that had to be provided rest while on a march and replacements were necessary in times of war.

question: To which Lodi Sultan does the given map related and what town does the site marked A on the map represent? (UPSC Prelims 1999)
option_1: Behlol Lodi, Jaunpur
option_2: Sikandar Lodi, Aligarh
option_3: Ibrahim Lodi, Jaunpur
option_4: Ibrahim Lodi, Aligarh
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

The correct option is (a) Behlol Lodi, Jaunpur.

  • The map shows a region of India, and the question asks to identify the Lodi Sultan associated with it and the town marked 'A'.
  • The conquest of the Jaunpur Sultanate was a significant event during the reign of Bahlol Lodi, the founder of the Lodi dynasty. He spent a considerable amount of time fighting against the Sharqi dynasty of Jaunpur and eventually annexed it, placing his eldest surviving son, Barbak, on the throne of Jaunpur in 1486.
  • Therefore, the map depicting the region of Jaunpur would be related to Bahlol Lodi, and the site marked 'A' would represent Jaunpur.

question: Who among the following streamlined the Maratha Administration after Sam bhaji ? (UPSC Prelims 2000)
option_1: Raja Ram
option_2: Balaji Vishwanath
option_3: Ganga Bai
option_4: Nanaji Deshmukh
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • Balaji Vishwanath is considered to have streamlined the Maratha administration after Sambhaji.
  • Balaji Vishwanath was the first Peshwa of the Maratha Empire and served as the chief administrator and advisor to the Maratha king. He also played a key role in Shahu’s final victory by winning over almost all the Maratha sardars to the side of Shahu.
  • Balaji Vishwanath died in April 1720 and was succeeded by his son, Baji Rao I, who was appointed Peshwa by Shahu. Bajirao is credited with expanding the Maratha Empire tenfold from 3% to 30% of the modern Indian landscape during 1720 1740.

question: Which one of the following Muslim rul ers was hailed as the ‘Jagadguru’ by his Muslim subjects because of his belief in secularism? (UPSC Prelims 2000)
option_1: Husain Shah
option_2: Zain ul Abadin
option_3: Ibrahim Adil Shah
option_4: Mahumad II
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • Ibrahim Adil Shah II was a Muslim ruler who was given the title of “Jagadguru Badshah” by people of all faiths in his kingdom. He was a member of the Adil Shah Dynasty and ruled the Sultanate of Bijapur from 1556–1627.
  • He was given this title because he treated all religions equally, sheltered Hindu scholars in his court, and renovated Hindu temples in his fort. He was also a skilled administrator, artist, poet, and patron of the arts.

question:
Match List I With List II and select the correct answer using the codes given be low the Lists: (UPSC Prelims 2000)

A. Iqta 

1. Marathas

B. Jagir

2. Delhi Sultans

C.Amaram

3. Mughals

D.Mokasa

4. Vijaygandara

 

 

 

 

 

Codes :

A B C D
option_1: 3 2 1 4
option_2: 2 3 4 1
option_3: 2 3 1 4
option_4: 3 2 4 1
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • Shamsa ud-din Iltutmish of the Delhi Sultanate introduced the Iqta system in India. The system was based on the ideas of Muhammad Ghori and was already used in Persia and the Middle East.
  • The Iqta system was a powerful tool for centralization. It divided the empire's area into portions, or "Iqta", and gave them to the king's troops, nobility, and officials. The system's main function was to collect taxes by Muqtis/Iqtedars.

The Iqta system had some pros and cons:

Pros:

  • Helped strengthen the Turko-Afghan powers, allowed commanders to command duties, and maintained good central control.

Cons:

  • Ran smoothly under strong Sultans, but problems arose under weak or greedy Sultans. After the passing of a powerful monarch, many Iqtadars would declare their freedom from the Sultan.

Jagirdar System:

  • The Delhi Sultanate introduced the jagirdar system in the early 13th century, and it continued during the Mughal Empire. The Jagirdari system was a land tenancy and feudal system.
  • The Jagirdari system was part of the mansabdari system, which developed under Akbar.
  • The system involved giving lands, or "jagirs", to Mansabdars in place of salary. The officers who received the lands were called jagirdars. The position of jagirdars was not hereditary.
  • During the Mughal Empire, the jagirdar collected taxes, which paid his salary and the rest to the Mughal treasury. The administration and military authority was given to a separate Mughal appointee.
  • The Jagirs were transferable, and no Jagirdar was given the same Jagir for a long time.
  • The system is said to have contributed to the weakening of the Mughal state.

Amaram System : 

  • The Vijayanagar Empire introduced the Amaram system. The Amaram system was a political innovation that involved granting land to military chiefs in exchange for their services. The land pieces were called Amaram. The top-grade officers of the army were known as Nayaks or Poligars.
  • The Amara-Nayakas were military commanders who governed territories. They collected taxes and other dues from the area's peasants, craftspeople, and traders. They kept some of the revenue for themselves and to maintain a set number of horses and elephants. In return, the Nayaks swore loyalty to the ruler and had to maintain a number of troops, horses, and elephants for the ruler's service.
  • The Amara-Nayaka system may have been influenced by the Delhi Sultanate's data System.

Mokasa System :

  • The Marathas introduced the Mokasa system.
  • The Mokasa system was part of the chauth levy, which helped the Marathas expand beyond the territories of Shivaji. The chauth levy was 25% of the gross revenue or produce from hostile or alien states.
  • The Marathas also levied a 10% tax on the collected chauth, called the sardeshmukhi.
  • The Marathas were known for their commando actions and well-organized army. Shivaji, the founder of the Maratha Empire, emphasized disciplined training, quality weaponry, and strategic planning.
  • The Marathas paid their ordinary soldiers in cash, but paid their military commander: and chiefs through jagir grants, also known as Mokasa or Saranjam. A Saranjam was a land grant given for military service or troop maintenance.

question: Which one of the Chola kings conquered Ceylon ? (UPSC Prelims 2001)
option_1: Aditya I
option_2: RajaRaja I
option_3: Rajendra
option_4: Vijaylaya
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

Rajendra Chola I (1014-44 AD) :

  • conquered Ceylon in 1017 CE and incorporated it into the Chola Empire.
  • Rajendra was a successful monarch and military general who continued his father’s policy of expansion and conquest. He also invaded the Srivijaya kingdom in Southeast Asia and established settlements in Kedah (Malaysia), Thailand, and Indonesia.
  • The Cholas controlled Sri Lanka until 1070, when Vijayabahu I defeated and expelled them.

Reason (R): Rana Sanga, the rajput hero was certainly a more formidable adversary than Ibrahim Lodi (UPSC Prelims 2001)
option_1: Both A and R individually true and R is the correct explanation of A

question: The shaded area in the above map shows the empire of.......... (UPSC Prelims 2001)
option_1: Ala ud din Khilji

option_3: Shah Jahan
option_4: Aurangzeb

question: Who among the following presided over the Buddhist council held during the re gion of Kanishka at Kashmir? (UPSC Prelims 2001)
option_1: Parsava
option_2: Nagarjuna
option_3: Sudraka
option_4: Vasumitra
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • Vasumitra presided over the Fourth Buddhist Council, which was held in 72 AD at Kundalvana, Kashmir. Asvaghosa was his deputy.
  • The council was held under the patronageof Kushan king Kanishka. During this council, the Mahayana and Hinayana sects of Buddhism were separated.

question:
Match List I with List II and select the correct answer using the code given be low the list: (UPSC Prelims 2001)

List - I 

List - II

(Bhakti Saint) 

 (Profession)

A. Namdev 

1.         Barber

B. Kabir 

2.         Weaver

C. Ravidas 

3.         Tailor

D. Sena 

4.         Cobbler

Codes :

A B C D
option_1: 2 3 1 4
option_2: 3 2 4 1
option_3: 3 2 1 4
option_4: 2 3 4 1
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

Saint Namdev :

  • He was a tailor by profession. He was a devotee of Lord Vitthala of Pandharpur and a member of the Varakari Sect.
  • Namdev was a well-known religious writer from Maharashtra and a companion saint poet of Saint Dnyaneshwar. He was one of the first writers to use the Marathi dialect in his work. He is considered to be a strong follower of the Bhagwat-Dharma, which has influence in Maharashtra and Punjab. Namdev is also known for introducing a tradition called Mahapurusha Sampradaya, which emphasized the cultural unity of the Marathas.

Kabir (1398–1518) :

  • He was a weaver by profession. He was also a renowned 15th century Indian saint, poet, and social reformer.
  • Kabir was born in Varanasi and raised by a Muslim couple who were also weavers. He became a disciple of the Hindu ascetic Ramananda, and is considered both a Sufi and Brahmin saint. Kabir’s poetry draws on both Hinduism and Islam, though he was critical of certain aspects of both faiths.
  • Kabir is respected by Hindus, Muslims, and Sikhs. His writings influenced Hinduism’s Bhakti movement, a religious movement in the medieval period that brought religious reforms by adopting the method of devotion to achieve salvation.

Ravidas (15th–16th century) :

  • He was a cobbler by profession. He was also a poet, devotee, and Gurmukh.
  • Ravidas was born in Varanasi to an untouchable leather-working caste. He spent most of his life with Sufi saints, sadhus, and ascetics. His poems and songs often centered around his low social position.
  • Ravidas was a prominent saint of the Indian Bhakti tradition. He became a disciple of Swami Ramananda of Varanasi and is said to have a direct intuitive vision of the Supreme self. Ravidassias Sikhs believe that Ravidas is their Guru (saint).
  • The Khalsa Sikhs have traditionally considered him one of many bhagats (holy person).

Sena Nhavi :

  • He is also known as Sant Sena Maharaj, was a barber by profession. He was a member of the Varkari sect, which is dedicated to Lord Vithoba. He was also a disciple of Saint Ramananda.
  • Sena Nhavi was a pious and God-loving man who lived near Pandharapur. He was so immersed in the worship of Lord Vitthal that he once incurred the wrath of a local king. According to legend, Lord Vitthal himself appeared to pacify the king and rescue Sena Nhavi.
  • In an abhanga, Sena also refers to his profession as a barber. He sang that barbers are skilled in the “art of shaving” and support the four caste-system (Varna (Hinduism)).

question: Who among the following Indian rulers established embassies in foreign coun tries on modern lines? (UPSC Prelims 2001)
option_1: Haider Ali
option_2: Mir Quasim
option_3: Shah Alam II
option_4: Tipu Sultan
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • Tipu Sultan established embassies in France, Turkey, and Egypt on modern lines. He was also known for his efforts to establish diplomatic relations with foreign powers.
  • Tipu Sultan, also known as the Tiger of Mysore, ruled the kingdom of Mysore from 1782 to 1799. He is known for his valiant acts in several wars and for saving his land from foreign invaders.

question: Which among the following ports was called Babul Makka (Gate of Makka) during the Mughal Period? (UPSC Prelims 2001)
option_1: Calicut
option_2: Broach
option_3: Cambay
option_4: Surat
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • Babul Makka, which translates to “Gate of Makka” or “Gateway to Makka”, is a name given to the port of Surat during the Mughal period. The name was given to Surat due to its role in international trade and its connection to the pilgrimage route to the holy city of Makka (Mecca) in Saudi Arabia.
  • Surat was also known as the gateway of West Asia as it was a main hub for goods and clothing traded via sea routes to other Asian countries.

question: The Mongols under Gengis Khan invaded India during the reign of.......... (UPSC Prelims 2001)
option_1: Balban
option_2: Feroz Tughlaq
option_3: Iltutmish
option_4: Mohammad bin Tughlaq
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • The Mongols under Genghis Khan invadedIndia during the reign of Iltutmish. Iltutmish was a Mamluk king who ruled the former Ghurid territories in northern India from 1211–1236.
  • The Mongol Empire invaded the Indian subcontinent from 1221–1327. They conquered the area around the Indus River and crossed into Punjab. However, the Mongols did not enter deep into India because Iltutmish refused to give shelter to the Persian king Khwarizm Shah, who Genghis Khan was chasing.

question:

question: The historian Barani refused to consider the state in India under Delhi Sultans as truly Islamic because.......... (UPSC Prelims 2002)
option_1: The majority of the population did not follow islam
option_2: The muslim thelogists were often disegrated
option_3: The Sultan supplemented the muslim law by farming his own regulation
option_4: Religious freedom was accorded to non muslim
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

The historian Ziauddin Barani refused to consider the state in India under the Delhi Sultans as truly Islamic for a few reasons:

  • The Sultan supplemented Muslim law by creating his own regulations.
  • Barani was a contemporary of Muhammad Tughlaq and Firoz Tughlaq, and enjoyed the patronage of both sultans. He is best known for composing Taarikh-i-Firoz Shahi, a work on medieval India.

question: With reference to medieval Indian rulers, Which one of the following statement is correct? (UPSC Prelims 2002)
option_1: Alauuddin Khilji first set up a seprate Ariz’s department
option_2: Balban introduced the branding system of horses of military
option_3: Mohammad bin Tughlaq was succeeed by his uncle to the Delhi throne
option_4: Firoz Tughlaq set up a seprate department of slaves
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • The Ariz-i-Mamalik or Dewan-i-Arz was the military department, and it was established much earlier, during the Slave dynasty, not by Alauddin Khilji. It existed during the reign of Balban, who served as Ariz-i-Mamalik before becoming Sultan.
  • Alauddin Khilji, however, reformed existing departments, especially those related to market control, intelligence, and military recruitment, but he did not establish a new Ariz’s department. Hence, statement (a) is incorrect.
  • The branding system of horses (Dagh system) was not introduced by Balban. It was introduced by Alauddin Khilji and later improved by Muhammad bin Tughlaq.
  • This system ensured that soldiers did not present inferior or fake horses during inspections. Each horse was branded with a specific mark to prevent corruption in the army. Therefore, attributing this reform to Balban is wrong, as it rightly belongs to Alauddin Khilji. Thus, statement (b) is incorrect. 

Statement (c) is also incorrect :

  •  After Muhammad bin Tughlaq (ruled 1325–1351 CE) died, he was not succeeded by his uncle, but by his cousin Firoz Shah Tughlaq. Firoz Shah was the son of Rajab, who was Muhammad bin Tughlaq’s father’s younger brother. So, the correct relation between them is cousins, not uncle and nephew.
  • Firoz Shah Tughlaq (ruled 1351–1388 CE) established a special department called Dewan-i-Bandagan, which managed the affairs of slaves (bandagan) under the Sultan. He is known to have kept thousands of slaves who were employed in administration, construction works, and royal service.
  • Apart from this, he also established other departments like Dewan-i-Khairat (for charity and dowries for poor girls) and Dewan-i-Kohi (for agricultural development). His reign is noted for administrative reforms and public welfare measures rather than military conquest. Thus, statement d is correct.

question:
With reference to Sufism in Indian history, consider the following statement: (UPSC Prelims 2002)

1. Shaikh Ahamad Surhandi was a contemporary of Ibrahim Lodi

2. Shaikh Nasruddin Chirag -i-Dehlavi was a disciple of Shaikh Nizamauddin Auliya

3. Aurangzeb was contemporary of Shaikh Salim Chisti

4. The Qadiri order of sufis was first introduced in India by Shaikh Niamtulhha and Makhdum Muhammad Jilani

Which of these statement are correct?
option_1: 1 and 2
option_2: 1 and 3
option_3: 2 and 3
option_4: 2 and 4
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • Shaykh Ahmad Sirhindi (1564-1624) was an Indian Sufi scholar, mystic, and theologian. He was a contemporary of Akbar and Jahangir. He is credited with reviving orthodox Sunnite Islam in India, which was a reaction to the religioustendencies during the reign of the Mughal emperor Akbar.
  • Nasiruddin Mahmud Chirag-Dehlavi was a 14th-century mystic poet and a Sufi saint of the Chishti Order. He was a disciple of Nizamuddin Auliya and later became his successor. Chiragh-e-Dehli translates to ‘The Illuminated Lamp of Delhi’.
  • Aurangzeb being a contemporary of Shaikh Salim Chisti is incorrect. Shaikh Salim Chisti was a revered Sufi saint during Akbar`s reign, much before Aurangzeb’s time.
  • The Qadiri order of Sufis being first introduced in India by Shaikh Niamtullah and Makhdum Muhammad Jilani is correct. They were instrumental in bringing this order of Sufism into India.

question: Who among the following was the first Bhakti Saint to use Hindi for the propa gation of his message ? (UPSC Prelims 2002)
option_1: Dadu
option_2: Kabir
option_3: Ramananda
option_4: Tulsidas
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • Ramananda (1360-1470) was the first Bhakti saint to use Hindi to spread his message. He was a devotional poet and saint of the Vaishnava sect in Northern India.
  • Ramananda is known for communicating in vernacular Hindi and accepting disciples of all castes. He also accepted disciples without discrimination based on their gender, class, caste, or religion. Many of his disciples were Muslims.
  • Ramananda is credited with popularizing the Bhakti movement in North India. He popularized the devotion of Ram and Sita. Kabir is one of his twelve most well-known students.

Kabir (1440–1518) :

  • He was an Indian poet and saint who is revered by Hindus, Muslims, and Sikhs. His writings influenced Hinduism’s Bhakti movement, and his verses are found in Sikhism’s scripture Guru Granth Sahib. Kabir’s work is read globally and has been alluded to in many Bollywood films.
  • Kabir is considered both a Sufi and Brahmin saint. He is said to have been born in or near Benares to a family of Muslim weavers before becoming a disciple of the Hindu ascetic Ramananda.
  • Kabir’s full name was Kabir Das.
  • The religious community Kabir Pant defines Kabir as the creator of Sant Mat sects, which are spread across central and North India.

Tulsidas (1511-1623) :

  • He was a Vaishnava Hindu poet and saint. His real name was Rambola Dubey. He is known for his devotion to the deity Rama. His main work, Ramcharitmanas, is a Hindi poem that is the most popular version of the story of Rama.

Dadu Dayal (1544–1603) :

  • He was a poet-saint, religious reformer, and mystic in the Bhakti tradition from Gujarat, India. His name means “the brother of compassion”.
  • Dadu Dayal was born in Ahmedabad in 1544 and settled in Rajasthan, where he became a traveling ascetic and religious preacher. He was a contemporary of Emperor Akbar, who is said to have been one of his followers. Dadu Dayal is known for speaking against formalism and priestcraft, and for opposing the Vedas and external form of worship. He attained Samadhi in Naraina in Jaipur district in 1601, and established the Dadupanth in his lifetime.

question: The motive behind Shahajan’s Balkh campaign was to........... (UPSC Prelims 2002)
option_1: Secure a friendly ruler in Balkh and Badakhshan which borderd Kabul
option_2: Conquer Samarquand and Farghana, the Mughal homelands
option_3: Fix the mughal frontier on the ‘scientific line’ the Amu Daria
option_4: Expand the Mughal frontier
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • The motive behind Shah Jahan’s Balkh campaign was to secure a friendly ruler in Balkh and Badakhshan, which bordered Kabul. The goal was to establish a stable and loyal administration in those areas to ensure the security and stability of Mughal territories in the region.
  • Shah Jahan was a Mughal emperor from 1628 to 1658. He is known for the grand monuments constructed during his reign, including the Taj Mahal and the Motī Masjid (Pearl Mosque) in Agra and the Jāmiṣ Masjid and Red Fort in Delhi.

question:
Assertion (A): Mohammad Bin Tughlaq left Delhi and for two years lived in a camp called Swargadwari

Reason (R) : At that time, Delhi was ravaged by a form of plague and many people died. (UPSC Prelims 2002)
option_1: Both A and R are individually true and R is the correct explanation of A
option_2: Both A and R are individually true and R is the correct explanation of A
option_3: A is true but R is False
option_4: A is false but R is true
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • Mohammad -bin-Tughlaq was the Sultan of Delhi from 1325 to 1351. From his accession to the throne in 1325 until his death in 1351, Muhammad contended with 22 rebellions, pursuing his policies, consistently and ruthlessly. He left Delhi for two years, lived in a camp called Swarga - dwari.
  • This was because at that time Delhi was ravaged by a form of plague(bubonic) and many people died. It wiped almost half of Sultan’s army. The plague attacked the city in 1334.

question:
Consider The Following Statements : (UPSC Prelims 2003)

1. The Cholas Defeated Pandya and Chera rulers and established there domination over penisular India in the early medieval times

2. The Cholas sent an expedition against Sailendra Empire of South East Asia and conqured some of the areas 

Which of these statement is/are correct?
option_1: Only 1
option_2: Only 2
option_3: Both 1 and 2
option_4: Niether 1 Nor 2
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • The Cholas defeated the Pandya and Chera rulers and established their dominance over peninsular India in the early medieval times.
  • The Chola Dynasty was at the height of its power and influence during the medieval period. The second Chola king, Aditya I, defeated the Pandyan Dynasty of Madurai in 885.
  • The most famous king of early Cholas is Karikala Chola, who ruled around 270 BC. He is known for winning the Battle of Venni, where he defeated the Pandyas and Cheras with the support of eleven Velir chieftains.
  • The Cholas remained stable until 1215, when they were absorbed by the Pandyan empire and ceased to exist by 1279.
  • In 1025, the Medieval Chola Emperor Rajendra I sent a naval expedition to Indochina, Indonesia, and the Malay Peninsula to subdue Srivijaya. This expedition is known as the “Rajendra Chola Expedition”.
  • The expedition resulted in the Cholas controlling parts of present-day Myanmar, Malaysia, and Indonesia. It also established the Chola Navy’s military capabilities and dominance in the region.
  • The expedition is considered unusual and mysterious in South Asian history. It is the only large-scale long-distance naval attacklaunched from South Asia in recorded history.
  • The Shailendra empire was based in Java and spread to other parts of Southeast Asia, including Sumatra, Bali, and parts of modern-day Malaysia and Thailand. The Shailendra dynasty declined in the 9th century, but their influence on Southeast Asian culture and religion continued.

question: How did Sultan Qutub-ud-din Aibak die? (UPSC Prelims 2003)
option_1: He was treacherously stabbed to death by one of his ambitious nobles
option_2: He was killed in a battle with Taj u din Yildiz, the rural of Ghazni who entered into a contest with him over the capture of Punjab
option_3: He sustined injuries while besieng the fortress of Kalinjar in Bundelkhand and succumbed to them later
option_4: He died after a fall from his horse while playing Chaugan
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • In 1210, Sultan Qutb-ud-din Aibak died after falling from his horse while playing chaugan, a form of polo on horseback. The pommel of the saddle pierced his ribs, and he died instantly. He was buried in Lahore, Pakistan.
  • Aibak was a fierce warrior who oversaw many battles during his life. Contemporary chroniclers praise him as a loyal, generous, courageous, and just man. He was succeeded by Aram Shah, and then by his former slave Iltutmish.

question: Which one of the following statement is not correct ? (UPSC Prelims 2003)
option_1: Ali Mardan Khan introduced the system of revenue farming in Bengal
option_2: Maharaja Ranjit singh set up modern foundries  to manufacture cannons at Lahore
option_3: Sawai Jai Singh Of Amber had Euclid Elements of Geometry translated in to Sanskrit
option_4: Sultan Tipu of Mysore gave money for the construction of the idol of godness Sarda in the Shringeri temple
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • Ali Mardan Khan did not introduce the system of revenue farming in Bengal. Instead, Murshid Quli Khan, also known as Mohammad Hadi, is credited with introducing the system during his tenure as the first Nawab of Bengal from 1717–1727.
  • The revenue farming system was introduced during the reign of the Mughal emperor Jahandar Shah. Under this system, the revenue farmer kept nine-tenths of the collection as his collection charges and paid the government the remaining tenth.
  • Murshid Quli Khan also reorganized Bengal’s finances by transferring large parts of jagir lands into Khalisah lands. He also recruited revenue farmers and officials from local merchant-bankers and zamindars. Maharaja
  • Ranjit Singh set up modern foundries to manufacture cannons at Lahore. Ranjit Singh was the founder and maharaja (1801–39) of the Sikh kingdom of the Punjab. He invested in infrastructure in the 1800s, establishing raw materials mines, cannon foundries, gunpowder, and arm factories. He also employed educated Muslims from Lahore and nearby cities to help him set up the factories.
  • Ranjit Singh also set up a series of gunpowder and shot (the precursor of the modern bullet) “karkhanas”. He employed Muslim gunners to man the foundries.
  • Sawai Jai Singh II (1688-1743) had Euclid’s Elements of Geometry translated into Sanskrit. Sawai Jai Singh was a Rajput king and a scholar of mathematics and astronomy. He was also a town planner and built the city of Jaipur.
  • Sawai Jai Singh also commissioned the Jantar Mantar observatories, which are a collection of large astronomical instruments used to calculate the movement of celestial bodies and local time.
  • Tipu Sultan, a Muslim ruler, gave money for the construction of the goddess Sharda temple in Sringeri after it was looted by Maratha horsemen in 1791. The temple is now home to the damaged Sharada Image.
  • Tipu Sultan’s close relationship with Sringeri began in 1785 when he issued a decree regranting the Shringeri Matha.
  • In addition to funds, Tipu also sent a letter requesting the Jagadguru to perform Hindu worship and penance for “good showers and crops”.
  • Tipu Sultan also donated a sandalwood idol of Sharada, a silver palanquin, and a spatika linga.

question: The Battle of Dharmat was fought between............ (UPSC Prelims 2003)
option_1: Muhammad Ghori and Jai Chand
option_2: Babur and the Afghans
option_3: Aurangzeb and Dara Shikoh
option_4: Ahmad Shah Durrani and the Marathas
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • The Battle of Dharmat was fought on April 15, 1658 between Aurangzeb and Jaswant Singh Rathore, who was allied with the Mughal prince Dara Shikoh.
  • The battle took place on the open plain of Dharmat, near Indore in central India.
  • The battle was a significant event that determined the fate of the Mughal Empire.
  • It was fought between two brothers, Aurangzeb and Dara Shikoh, who were both vying for the Mughal throne after the death of their father, Emperor Shah Jahan, in 1666.
  • The two armies were evenly matched, with Aurangzeb commanding 30,000 troops and Dara Shikoh commanding 40,000 troops.
  • Aurangzeb won a decisive victory due to advantage in artillery and tactics.

question: Alam Khan, one of those who invited Babur to invade India was.............. (UPSC Prelims 2003)
option_1: An uncle of Ibrahim Lodi and a pretender to the throne of Delhi
option_2: A cousin of Ibrahim Lodi who was illtreated and expelled from the country
option_3: The father of Dilawar Khan to whom cruel treatment was meted out by Ibrahim Lodi
option_4: A high official in punjab province who was very much disconnected with Ibrahim Lodi's treatment to his tribe
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • Alam Khan, also known as Alauddin Shah, was one of the people who invited Babur to invade India. Alam Khan was the uncle of Ibrahim Lodhi, the Sultan of Delhi, and a pretender to the throne. He wanted to rule Delhi and conspired with Daulat Khan Lodhi against Ibrahim Lodhi.
  • According to some sources, Alam Khan and Daulat Khan Lodi invited Babur to India. In 1526, Babur defeated Ibrahim Lodi in the first battle of Panipat.
  • Alam Khan and Babur signed a treaty in Kabul. The terms of the treaty were that Babur would provide military assistance to Alam Khan so that he could secure the throne of Delhi. In return, Alam Khan would accept Babur’s legal ownership over the areas of Punjab.

question: In India among the following location the Dutch established there earlist factory at............. (UPSC Prelims 2003)
option_1: Surat
option_2: Pulicat
option_3: Cochin
option_4: Cassimbazar
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • The Dutch established their first factory in India in 1605 at Masulipatnam in Andhra Pradesh.

They later established factories at many other locations, including:

  •  Pulicat: 1610
  •  Surat: 1616
  •  Bimilipatam: 1641
  •  Karikal: 1645
  •  Chinsura: 1653
  • The Dutch also established trading centers in other parts of India.

question: How did the Mughal emperor Jahandar Shah's reign come to an early end........... (UPSC Prelims 2003)
option_1: He was deposed by his Wazir
option_2: He died due to a sleep while climbing down steps
option_3: He was detected by his nepew in a battle
option_4: He died of sickness due to much consumption of wine
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • The Mughal Emperor Jahandar Shah’s reign ended in January 1713 when he was defeated in battle by his nephew, Farrukhsiyar. Jahandar Shah’s reign was marked by incompetence and misrule.

Here’s what happened:

1. Jahandar Shah became the Mughal Emperor in 1712 after the death of his father, Bahadur Shah I.

2. In 1712, Jahandar Shah ascended the throne by defeating Farrukhsiyar’s father, Azim-ush-Shan.

3. Farrukhsiyar wanted revenge for his father’s death and was joined by Hussain Ali Khan and Abdullah Khan.

4. On January 10, 1713, Jahandar Shah was defeated by Farrukhsiyar in a battle at Agra.

5. Jahandar Shah fled to Delhi, where he was captured and handed over to the new Emperor.

6. Jahandar Shah was confined for a month, until February 11, 1713, when professional stranglers were sent to murder him.

question:
Assertion (A) : Saluva Narsimha put an end to the old dynasty and assumed the royal title.

Reason (R): He wanted to save the kingdom from further degenration and disintegration (UPSC Prelims 2003)
option_1: Both A and R are individually true and R is the correct explanation of A
option_2: Both A and R are individually true and R is the correct explanation of A
option_3: A is true but R is false
option_4: A is false but R is true
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • Saluva Narasimha Deva Raya, who served as a powerful general and minister under the weakening Sangama dynasty of the Vijayanagara Empire, did indeed put an end to the old dynasty and assumed the royal title around 1485 CE, thereby establishing the Saluva dynasty. This makes the Assertion (A) true. 
  • The empire during this period was facing political instability, internal revolts, and external invasions by the Bahmanis and Gajapatis, which created conditions ripe for a strong military leader to seize control.
  • However, the Reason (R) given — that he took power to save the kingdom from further degeneration and disintegration — is not fully supported by historical evidence.
  • Although Saluva Narasimha may have claimed to act in the interest of preserving the empire, there is no conclusive proof that this was his principal or only motivation. 
  • His assumption of the throne was more likely driven by the collapse of central authority and the opportunity to stabilize power under his leadership. 
  • Therefore, while the assertion that he ended the old dynasty is factually correct, the stated reason oversimplifies his complex political motives and cannot be considered entirely true. Hence, A is true but R is false.

question:
Assertion (A) : Emperor Akbar marched towards Afganistan in 1581 with a huge army.

Reason (R): He was on his way to reclaim his ancestral country of Ferghana in Central Asia. (UPSC Prelims 2003)
option_1: Both A and R are individually true and R is not the correct explanation of A
option_2: Both A and R are individually true but R is not the correct explanation of A
option_3: A is true but R is false
option_4: A is false but R is true
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • The assertion that Emperor Akbar marched towards Afghanistan in 1581 with a huge army is true. However, the reason that he was on his way to reclaim his ancestral country of Ferghana in Central Asia is incorrect.
  • Akbar marched towards Afghanistan in 1581 to conquer Kabul and expand his kingdom. In 1581-82, Akbar faced a crisis when disgruntled nobles and officers declared his half brother Mirza Hakim as their ruler. Mirza Hakim was the ruler of Kabul and he revolted against Akbar aiming Delhi Throne.
  • Akbar reigned from 1556 to 1605 and extended Mughal power over most of the Indian subcontinent.

question: How did the dynasty of Nizam Shahis of Ahmadnagar come to an end.......... (UPSC Prelims 2004)
option_1: Ahmadnagar was annexed into Mughal empire and Husain Shah was consigned to life imprisonment
option_2: Mughal troops destroyed Daulatabad fort and killed Nizam ul Mulk of Ahamadnagar
option_3: Fateh Khan usurped the throne from Nizam ul Mulk
option_4: Malik Ambar was defected in a battle with mughals in 1631 and the entire royal familly was killed by the mughal troops
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • The Nizam Shahi dynasty of Ahmadnagar came to an end in 1633 when the Mughals annexed Ahmadnagar and imprisoned its last ruler, Husain Shah.

Here are some events that led to the end of the Nizam Shahi dynasty:

  •  1596: The Mughals attack from the north, and Chand Bibi, queen dowager of Bijapur, resists.
  •  1600: Ahmadnagar falls after the queen’s death.
  •  1633: The Nizam Shahi dynasty survives until the fall of Daulatabad.
  •  1633: Burhan Nizam Shah II, Husain Shah’s successor, tries to rebel against the Mughals but is defeated and killed in battle.
  • 1633: Husain Shah is accused of conspiring against the Mughals and is imprisoned for life.
  • The Nizam Shahi dynasty was established in 1490 by Malik Ahmad, the Bahmani governor of Junnar. The Deccan Sultanates, including Ahmadnagar, were conquered by the Mughal Empire between 1616 and 1636.

question:
Consider the following statement : (UPSC Prelims 2004)

1. Narsimha Saluva ended the Sangama dynasty and seized the throne for himself and started the Saluva dynasty.

2. Vira Narsimha deposed the last Saluva ruler and seized the throne for himself.

3. Vira Narasimha was succeeded by his younger brother, Krishnadeva Raya.

4. Krishnadeva Raya was succeeded by his halfbrother, Achyuta Raya.

Which of the statement given above are correct ?
option_1: 1, 2, and 3
option_2: 2, 3, and 4
option_3: 1 and 4
option_4: 1, 2, 3 and 4
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • Narasimha Saluva ended the Sangama dynasty and established the Saluva dynasty.
  • Narasimha Saluva defeated Dev Raya-II of the Sangama dynasty to establish the Saluva dynasty. The Saluva dynasty ruled the Vijayanagara Empire from 1485 to 1505.
  • Vira Narasimha deposed the last Saluva ruler and seized the throne for himself. Vira Narasimha was succeeded by his younger brother, Krishnadeva Raya. Krishnadeva Raya was succeeded by his half brother, Achyuta Raya.

question: Who was the last ruler of the Tughlaq dynasty.............. (UPSC Prelims 2004)
option_1: Firoz Shah Tughlaq
option_2: GhiyasuddinTughlaq II
option_3: Mahmud Shah Tughlaq
option_4: Nusarat Shah
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • Nasir-ud-Din Mahmud Shah Tughlaq (1394–1413) was the last ruler of the Tughlaq dynasty. He was also known as Nasiruddin Mohammad Shah and the Darvesi King.
  • Nasir-ud-Din Mahmud Shah Tughlaq was the last sultan of the Islamic Delhi Sultanate. In 1398, Timur invaded India during his reign, and the Tughlaq dynasty ended soon after. The next sultan of the Delhi Sultanate was the first of the Sayyid Dynasty.

Firoz Shah Tughlaq (1309 - 1388) :

  • He was a Muslim ruler from the Tughlaq dynasty who ruled the Sultanate of Delhi from 1351 to 1388. He is known as the father of the irrigation approach in India and is considered the third and most powerful king of the Tughlaq dynasty.

Here are some of his achievements:

  •  Irrigation: Built the biggest irrigation network of his time.
  •  Qutub Minar: Repaired the Qutub Minar after it was damaged by an earthquake.
  •  Islamic Law: Encouraged the study of Islamic Law and compiled many books on the subject.
  •  Taxes: Imposed taxes including Kharaj, Zakat, Kham, and Jaziya.
  •  Infrastructure: Developed infrastructure in his kingdom.
  •  Inheritance: Provided the principle of inheritance to the armed forces.
  •  Land survey: Surveyed all farmland in the kingdom.
  •  Officer salaries: Paid officers through land revenue income of towns instead of real money.

Firoz Shah Tughlaq also:

  • Forbade Muslim women from visiting holy people’s graves to pray. Demolished Hindu temples, as well as their books and literature.

Nasiruddin Nasrat Shah (1518–1533) :

  • He was the second Sultan of Bengal and a member of the Hussain Shahi dynasty. He was born into a wealthy Sunni Muslim family and succeeded his father, Alauddin Husain Shah, in 1519.
  • Nasrat Shah continued his father’s expansionist policies, but by 1526, he had to contend with the Mughal ascendency in the Battle of Ghaghra.
  • He also extended his territory into Tirhut (northern Bihar) and established his headquarters at Hajipur. Nasrat Shah died in 1533.

question:
Consider the following statement about Sikh Gurus.............. (UPSC Prelims 2004)

1. Banda Bahadur was appointed as the military leader of the Sikhs By Guru Gobind Singh after Guru Ram Das

2. Guru Arjan Dev became the Sikh Guru after Guru Ram Das

3. Guru Arjan Dev gave to Sikhs their own sript Gurumukhi

Which one of the statement given above is/are correct ?
option_1: 1 only
option_2: 2 and 3
option_3: 1 and 3
option_4: 1 and 2
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • Guru Gobind Singh, the tenth Sikh Guru, appointed Baba Banda Singh Bahadur as the military leader of the Sikhs in 1708.
  • Banda Bahadur was a prominent figure in Sikh history. In the early 18th century, Sikhs faced intense persecution under Mughal rule. Banda Bahadur and his forces fought a series of military campaigns against the Mughal rulers and their allies. In 1710, he captured the city of Sirhind, which was known for its brutal treatment of Sikhs.
  • Banda Bahadur was a follower of Guru Gobind Singh and converted from Hinduism to become a Sikh. He left home at the age of 15 after seeing a dying deer, and became a yogi. Guru Gobind Singh made him realize he was a Sikh and needed to fight for them.

Banda Bahadur fought in the following battles:

1. Battle of Sonipat

2. Battle of Samana

3. Battle of Kapuri

4. Battle of Sadhaura

5. Battle of Ropar (1710)

6. Battle of Chappar Chiri

7. Battle of Sirhind

8. Battle of Saharanpur

  • Guru Arjan Dev became the fifth Sikh Guru after the death of his father, Guru Ram Das, in 1581.
  • Guru Arjan Dev was born on April 15, 1563 in Goindwal Sahib, India. He played a significant role in shaping Sikhism as a religion and a way of life.
  • He was the first Guru to serve as both the temporal and spiritual head of the Sikhs. He also completed the Golden Temple at Amritsar, where all Sikhs could worship.
  • Guru Arjan Dev was executed by the fourth Mughal Ruler, Jahangir. After his execution, his son Hargobind became the sixth Guru in 1606.
  • Guru Angad Dev, the second Sikh Guru, invented the Gurmukhi script in the mid-16th century. The word "Gurmukhi" translates to "from the mouth of the guru". 
  • Guru Angad Dev taught and spread the Gurmukhi script at the suggestion of Guru Nanak Dev, the founder of Sikhism. 
  • Guru Angad Dev developed the Gurmukhi script from the Landa scripts to correct inadequacies in the Lahnda script. The Gurmukhi script is predominantly used in present-day Punjab, India. 
  • The Guru Granth Sahib, the holy book of the Sikh religion, is written in the Gurmukhi script.

question: Which one of the following sequences in dicates the correct chronological order : (UPSC Prelims 2004)
option_1: Shankaracharya - Ramanuja - Chaitanya
option_2: Ramanuja - Shankaracharya - Chaitanya
option_3: Ramanuja - Chaitanya - shankaracharya
option_4: Shankaracharya - Chaitanya - Ramanuja
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  •  Shankaracharya : Born on 788 AD at Kaladi. present day Kerala, India. One of the greatest philosophers of India, Adi Shankaracharya founded the Advaita Vedanta.
  •  Ramanuja : Ramanuja (traditionally, 1017-1137 AD) was Hindu theologian, philosopher, and one of the most important exponent of the Sri Vaishnavism tradition. within Hinduism. Ramanuja presented the epistemic (doctrine of salvation) and soteriological (ujh to knowledge) importance of bhakti.
  •  Chaitanya : Chaitanya Mahaprabhu (18 February 1486 – 14 June 1534) was a Bengali spiritual teacher who founded Gaudiya Vaishnavism.

question: Which one of the following is the correct chronological order of the Afghan rules to the throne of Delhi? (UPSC Prelims 2006)
option_1: Sikandar Shah - Ibrahim Lodi - Bahlol Khan Lodi
option_2: Sikandar Shah - Bahlol Khan Lodi - Ibrahim Lodi
option_3: Bahlol Khan Lodi - Sikandar Shah - Ibrahim Lodi
option_4: Bahol Khan Lodi - Ibrahim Lodi - Sikandar Shah
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

The correct chronological order of the Afghan rulers to the throne of Delhi is:

  • Bahlol (1451–1489)
  • Sikandar (1489–1517)
  • Ibrahim (1517–1526)
  • The Lodi dynasty was an Afghan dynasty that ruled the Delhi Sultanate from 1451 1526. The founder of the dynasty, Bahlul Khan Lodi, was a Khalji of the Lodi clan.

question: In Indian history, who was Abdul Hamid Lahori............ (UPSC Prelims 2006)
option_1: An important military commander during Akbar Reign
option_2: An official historian of the reign of Shah Jahan
option_3: An important noble and confidant of Aurangzeb
option_4: A chronicler and poet during the reign of Mohammad Shah
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • Abdul Hamid Lahori was a 17th century historian and traveler who lived during the reign of Mughal Emperor Shah Jahan.
  • He later became Shah Jahan’s court historian and is best known for his work Padshahnamah.
  • The book is divided into two volumes and is the official history of Shah Jahan’s reign.
  • It includes illustrated manuscript versions and describes Shah Jahan’s life and activities during the first twenty years of his reign.
  • Abdul Hamid Lahori may also refer to a soldier in the Indian Army who served in the 4th Battalion of the Grenadiers regiment. He joined the army in 1953 at the age of 20 and was posted to the 4th Battalion in 1955.
  • During the Indo-Pakistani War of 1965, Hamid destroyed eight Pakistani tanks and was killed in action while destroying the ninth.

question: Bhakta Tukaram was a contemporary of which Mughal Emperor? (UPSC Prelims 2006)
option_1: Babar
option_2: Akbar
option_3: Jahangir
option_4: Aurangzeb
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • Bhakta Tukaram was a contemporary of the Mughal Emperor Jahangir. Jahangir ruled from 1605 to 1627.
  • Tukaram was a Marathi Bhakti poet and devotee of Lord Krishna who lived from 1608 to 1649. He was a farmer’s son and a follower of the Varkari devotionalism tradition. He is known for his Abhyanga (devotional poetry) and kirtans (community-oriented worship with spiritual singing).

question:
Direction: The following question consists of two statement, one labeled as the As sertion (A) and the other as Reason (R) You are to examine these two statement carefully and select the answer to these item using the code given below: (UPSC Prelims 2006)

Assertion (A) : Muhammad bin Tughlaq issued a new gold coin which was called Dinar by Ibn Batutah.

Reason (R) : Muhammad bin Tughlaq wanted to issue token currency in gold coins to promote trade with West Asian and North African countries.
option_1: Both A and R are individually true and R is the correct explanation of A
option_2: Both A and R are individually true but R is not the correct explanation of A
option_3: A is true But R is false
option_4: A is false but R is true
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • The assertion is true, but the reason is false. This is the correct answer.
  • It is a historical fact that Mohammed bin Tughlaq issued a new gold coin called Dinar. However, the reason provided for this event is incorrect. Mohammad bin Tughlaq introduced token currency in the form of brass and copper coins, not gold.
  • The introduction of token currency was not specifically designed to promote trade with West Asian and North African countries.

question: When Raja Wodeyar founded the King dom of Mysore who was the ruler of the Vijaynagar Empire? (UPSC Prelims 2006)
option_1: Sadasiva
option_2: Tirumala
option_3: Ranga II
option_4: Venkata II
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • When Raja Wodeyar I (also known as Raja Wadiyar) established the independent Kingdom of Mysore in 1610 CE, the ruler of the Vijayanagar Empire was Venkata II (Venkata Deva Raya II) of the Aravidu dynasty.
  • After the decline of the Vijayanagar Empire following the Battle of Talikota in 1565, the empire’s power gradually weakened and its capital shifted to Chandragiri and later to Vellore.
  • During this period, several provincial chiefs and Nayakas who were earlier under Vijayanagar rule began asserting their independence. One such ruler was Raja Wodeyar I, who took advantage of the weakening central authority.
  • In 1610 CE, Raja Wodeyar I captured Srirangapatna from the local Vijayanagar governor (Tirumala, the viceroy under Venkata II) and made it his capital. This marked the beginning of an independent Mysore kingdom under the Wodeyars.

question: With whose permission did the English set up there first factory in Surat........... (UPSC Prelims 2009)
option_1: Akbar
option_2: Jahangir
option_3: Shahajan
option_4: Aurangzeb
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • The Mughal Emperor Jahangir permitted the British East India Company to establish their first permanent factory in Surat in 1613. The factory was opened on January 11, 1613.
  • The British East India Company was founded in India in 1600 and came to India to trade in spices. During Jahangir’s reign, the English factory in Surat faced challenges and conflicts with local powers and rival European trading companies.
  • In 1615, English diplomat Sir Thomas Roe presented his credentials to Jahangir as the Ambassador of the King of England. Jahangir granted him permission, or “firman”, to trade in Surat. This led to the establishment of the English factory at Surat on a permanent basis.

question: During the time of which Mughal Emper or did the english East India Company es tablish its first factory in India? (UPSC Prelims 2009)
option_1: Akbar
option_2: Jahangir
option_3: Shah Jahan
option_4: Aurangzeb
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • The English East India Company established its first factory in Surat in 1613 with the permission of Mughal Emperor Jahangir. The British East India Company came to India for trade in spices, which was an important commodity in Europe at the time.
  • The East India Company first visited Surat in 1608 to establish trade with India. The beginning and growth of the East India Company led to the establishment of factories at Surat, Ahmedabad, Agra, and Masulipatam.

question: What was the immediate reason for Ahmad Shah Abdali to invade India and fight the third battle of Panipat? (UPSC Prelims 2010)
option_1: He wanted to avenge the Expulsion by Marathas of his viceroy Tamur Shah from Lahore
option_2: The frustrated governor of Julundhar adina Beg Khan invited him to invade Punjab
option_3: He wanted to punish Mughal administration for non payment of the revenues of the Chaharmahal
option_4: He wanted to annex all the fertile paints of Punjab up to the borders of Delhi to his kingdom
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • Ahmad Shah Abdali, also known as Ahmad Shah Durrani, invaded India in October 1759 to avenge the Marathas’ expulsion of his viceroy, Timur Shah, from Lahore. The Marathas were gaining power in northern India and Abdali saw the expulsion as a direct challenge to his authority.

The Battle of Panipat :

  • It was a series of three military engagements that took place in northern India in 1526, 1556, and 1761. The battles were fought at Panipat, a plain that was suitable for cavalry movements.
  • The First Battle of Panipat took place on April 21, 1526. The battle was fought between the invading forces of Babur and the Lodi Empire. Babur’s forces defeated the much larger army of Ibrahim Lodi, Sultan of Delhi. The battle marked the beginning of the Mughal Empire and the end of the Delhi Sultanate.
  • Babur’s victory was due to his tactics and the use of guns. Babur’s new tactics included the Tulghuma and the Araba. The sound of the canons frightened Lodhi’s war elephants. The Lodi troops were also lacking in artillery skills.
  • The Second Battle of Panipat took place on November 5, 1556. The battle was fought between the forces of Samrat Hem Chandra Vikramaditya, also known as Hemu, and the army of Akbar. The battle was a decisive victory for Akbar’s generals Khan Zaman I and Bairam Khan.

Why All Battle Fought in Panipat Only?

  • Battles were fought in Panipat due to its strategic location, political significance, and neutral ground status.
  • Panipat is located on the Grand Trunk Road, a trade route that connects Central Asia and the Indian subcontinent. It’s also 90 kilometers north of Delhi, which has been the seat of the Indian ruler since the Chauhan Vansh.
  • Panipat’s climatic conditions are also suitable for long battles, with a short rainy season and a long dry season.
  • The battles of Panipat had a significant impact on India’s history and helped shape the country’s political and cultural landscape.

question: Among the following, who was not a proponet of bhakti cult?  (UPSC Prelims 2010)
option_1: Nagarjuna
option_2: Tukaram
option_3: Tyagarja
option_4: Valabhacharya
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • Nagarjuna was not a proponent of the Bhakti cult. Nagarjuna was a Buddhist philosopher and the founder of the Madhyamaka school of Mahayana Buddhism.
  • The Bhakti cult is a Hindu movement that emphasizes the mutual intense emotional attachment and love. The Bhakti movement was a religious revolution started by the Hindu saints. It had two divisions, the Nirguna and Saguna. The Bhakti movement influenced a large number of people in India.
  • Tukaram, Tyagaraja, and Vallabhacharya were all proponents of the Bhakti movement in India.

question:
Why did Buddhism start declining in In dia in the early medieval times?  (UPSC Prelims 2010)

1. Buddha was by that time considered as one of the incarnation of Vishnu and thus became a part of Vaishnavism

2. The invading tribes from central Asia till the time of last Gupta king adopted Hidusm and persecuted Buddhists

3. The Kings of Gupta dynasty were strongly opposed to Buddhism

Which of the statement given above is/are correct?
option_1: 1 Only
option_2: 1 and 3 only
option_3: 2 and 3 only
option_4: 1, 2 and 3
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • During the early medieval period, Buddhism began to lose its distinct identity as Buddha himself was absorbed into Hinduism. He was considered the ninth incarnation (avatar) of Lord Vishnu under Vaishnavism. This religious synthesis caused many followers to gradually merge back into Hinduism, leading to a decline in separate Buddhist institutions and teachings. Thus, this was one of the main reasons for the decline of Buddhism in India.
  • The Central Asian tribes, such as the Kushanas and later the Huns, did invade India, but it is incorrect to say that they “adopted Hinduism and persecuted Buddhists.” In fact, some rulers like Kanishka (a Kushana king) were great patrons of Buddhism, and Buddhism flourished under them. Though the Huns did cause destruction, their attacks affected many religious centers in general and not only Buddhist ones. Hence, this statement is not entirely true.
  • The Gupta kings were not opposed to Buddhism. They were primarily Hindus who followed Vaishnavism or Shaivism but were known for their religious tolerance. Buddhism and Jainism both received patronage during the Gupta period. Buddhist scholars like Vasubandhu and the flourishing of Nalanda University under Gupta rule show their liberal attitude. Therefore, it is wrong to say the Gupta rulers persecuted Buddhists or opposed Buddhism.
  • Only Statement 1 is correct because the assimilation of Buddha into Hinduism as an avatar of Vishnu reduced Buddhism’s separate identity.

Thus , the correct answer is (A)  1 Only

question:
With reference to the religious history of Medieval India, the Sufi mystics were known to pursue which of the following practices?  (UPSC Prelims 2012)

1. Meditation and control of breath

2. Severe ascetic exercises in a lonely place

3. Recitation of holy songs to arouse a state of ecstasy in their audience

Select the correct answer using the codes given below:
option_1: 1 and 2 only
option_2: 2 and 3 only
option_3: 3 only
option_4: 1, 2 and 3
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

The correct answer is (B) 2 and 3 only.

1. Meditation and control of breath:

  • This statement is not entirely correct for Sufi practices. Meditation and breath control (pranayama) are features more commonly associated with yogic and Hindu spiritual traditions. While Sufis did engage in meditation (muraqaba) and remembrance of God (zikr), their practices did not emphasize yogic breath control as a formal spiritual discipline. Hence, this statement is partially correct but not a defining Sufi feature, so it is excluded in the final answer.

2. Severe ascetic exercises in a lonely place:

  • This statement is correct.
  • Sufi mystics often practiced asceticism (zuhd) and solitude (khalwa) to purify their souls and detach from worldly desires. They believed spiritual progress required living a simple, disciplined, and detached life. Many Sufis meditated and prayed in isolation to strengthen their connection with God. Hence, this statement accurately represents an important Sufi practice.

3. Recitation of holy songs to arouse a state of ecstasy in their audience:

  • This statement is correct.
  • Sufis practiced sama‘ — the recitation or singing of devotional songs, hymns, or poetry — to create an atmosphere of spiritual ecstasy and divine love. This was meant to bring both the singer and listeners closer to God through intense emotional and spiritual experience.
  • In India, this tradition evolved into qawwali, popularized by Chishti Sufis like Khwaja Moinuddin Chishti and Amir Khusrau. Hence, this is an authentic and famous Sufi practice.

question:
Consider the following Bhakti Saints............. (UPSC Prelims 2013)

1. Dadu Dayal

2. Guru Nanak

3. Tyagaraja

Who among the above was/were preaching when the Lodi dynasty fell and Babur took over?
option_1: 1 and 3
option_2: 2 only
option_3: 2 and 3
option_4: 1 and 2
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  •  Guru Nanak is the only one of these Bhakti saints who was preaching when the Lodi dynasty fell and Babur took over:

Guru Nanak :

  • Born in 1469, Nanak was a Hindu who founded Sikhism and opposed idol worship and the caste system. He believed in the worship of one God through prayer and meditation.

Dadu Dayal :

  • A poet-saint and religious reformer from Gujarat, India, Dadu Dayal was a disciple of Kabir and a supporter of Hindu-Muslim unity. He criticized the practices of sati and purdah, and believed in a balanced life that was natural, harmonious, and chaste.

Tyagaraja :

  • A Carnatic music composer who dedicated his life to devotion and spirituality. He wrote thousands of “kritis” in praise of Lord Ram, many of which are still popular today.

question: Ibadat Khana at Fatehpur Sikri was............. (UPSC Prelims 2014)
option_1: The mosque for the use of Royal family
option_2: Akbar’s private prayer chamber
option_3: The hall in which Akbar held discussions with scholars of various religions
option_4: The room in which the nobles belonging to different religions gathered to discuss religious affairs
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • Akbar invited leaders, intellectuals, theologians, and religious leaders from different religions to discuss their opinions. The house was also used to teach that all religions are the same.
  • The Ibadat Khana has an octagonal platform in the center that was the seat of the emperor. The four ministers of the house, Abdur Rahim, Birbal, Faizi, and Abu'l Fazl, had different seats in the house.
  • The Ibadat Khana is part of Fatehpur Sikri, a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

question: In medieval India, the designations ‘Ma hattara’ and ‘Pattakila’ were used for............. (UPSC Prelims 2014)
option_1: Military officers
option_2: Village headmen
option_3: Specialists in Vedic rituals
option_4: Chiefs of craft guilds
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • In medieval India, the designations “Mahattara” and “Pattakila” were used for village headmen.
  • Village headmen were the leaders of their communities and owned most of the land.
  • They had many official duties and acted as mediators in disputes involving the village or individuals. They also acted as intermediaries between the villagers and higher authorities, representing the interests of the villagers and ensuring the smooth functioning of the village affairs.
  • While it is possible that these terms were used for military officers, it is not the most likely option. There were other specific terms for military officers in medieval India.

Terms used for military officers in medieval India:

  • Mahadandanayaka: or Mahabaladhikrita
  • Dandanayaka: or Baladhikrita
  • Nayaka
  • Gulmin

question:
Consider the following pairs: (UPSC Prelims 2015)

 

Medieval Indian State

Present Region

1.

Champaka

Central India

2.

Durgara

Jammu

3.

Kuluta

Malabar

Which of the above pairs is/are correctly matched?
option_1: 1 and 2
option_2: 2 only
option_3: 1 and 3
option_4: 3 only
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • The medieval Indian state of Champaka is present day Chamba in Himachal Pradesh.
  • Champaka is one of several medieval Indian states that remained outside the main areas of conflict in the northern plains.

Other states include:

  •  Durgara: Jammu
  •  Trigarta: Jalandhar
  •  Kuluta: Kulu, Himachal Pradesh
  • Kumaon
  • Garhwal
  • Champaka Sarasi is also the name of an ancient water tank near the town of Anandapura in the Shivamogga district of Karnataka.

question:
Consider the following: (UPSC Prelims 2015)

The arrival of Babur into India led to the

1. Introduction of gunpowder in the subcontinent

2. Introduction of the arch and dome in the region’s architecture

3. Establishment of Timurid dynasty in the region

Select the correct answer using the code given below:
option_1: 1 and 2 only
option_2: 3 only
option_3: 1 and 3 only
option_4: 1, 2 and 3
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)
