  • Babur was a descendant of the Timurid dynasty, a clan of Turco-Mongol lineage. The Timurid dynasty’s establishment in the region marked the beginning of the Mughal Empire.
  • However, some say that gunpowder was introduced to the subcontinent much earlier, probably in the 13th century by the Mongols. Chinese gunpowder technology is believed to have arrived in India by the mid-14th century.
  • The use of arches and domes became more prominent during the Mughal era, which followed Babur’s reign. Mughal architecture is characterized by the prominent use of arches and domes, and had a significant impact on the architectural style of the Indian subcontinent.

question:
With reference to Indian history, which of the following is/are the essential ele ment/elements of the feudal system? (UPSC Prelims 2015)

1. A very strong centralized political authority and a very weak provincial or local political authority

2. Emergence of administrative structure based on control and possession of land

3. Creation of lord-vassal relationship between the feudal lord and his overlord

Select the correct answer using the code given below:
option_1: 1 and 2 only
option_2: 2 and 3 only
option_3: 3 only
option_4: 1, 2 and 3
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • Feudalism is a political system of power dispersed and balanced between king and nobles.
  • Broadly defined, it was a way of structuring society around relationships derived from the holding of land in exchange for service or labour (lord-vassal relationship).
  • The feudal system in India was characterized by the emergence of an administrative structure based on land control and possession.
  • The feudal system was a set of legal and military customs in medieval Europe that flourished between the 9th and 15th centuries. It can be defined as a system for structuring society around relationships derived from the holding of land in return for services.
  • In the feudal system, landowners (lords) offered land tenure rights and protection to stewards (vassals), who in turn, pledged money and military assistance to the lords.

question: Who of the following founded a new city on the south bank of a tributary to river Krishna and undertook to rule his new kingdom as the agent of a deity to whom all the land south of the river Krishna was supposed to belong? (UPSC Prelims 2015)
option_1: Amoghavarsha I
option_2: Ballala II
option_3: Harihara I
option_4: Prataparudra II
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • Harihara I, also known as Hakka Raya, founded a new city on the south bank of the Tungabhadra River, a tributary of the Krishna River. He also ruled his new kingdom as the agent of a deity to whom all the land south of the Krishna River was supposed to belong.
  • Harihara I was the founder of the Vijayanagara Empire, which was established in 1336. The empire stretched from the Krishna River in the north to the extreme south of the peninsula.

question: With reference to the economic history of medieval India, the term ‘Araghatta’ refers to............. (UPSC Prelims 2016)
option_1: Bonded labour
option_2: Land grants made to military officers
option_3: Waterwheel used in the irrigation of land
option_4: Wasteland converted to cultivated land
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • The term “Araghatta” refers to a Persian wheel used in medieval India for irrigating land. It is a mechanical device that lifts water from open wells, typically operated by animals like camels, buffaloes, or bullocks.
  • The term “Araghatta” comes from the Sanskrit words “ara” and “ghatta”, which mean “spoke” and “pot” respectively. In ancient texts, the term has been used to describe the Persian wheel.
  • The irrigation system in medieval India was complex, using wheels to lift water from wells. The system used a garland of pots with three wheels, a gear system, and animal power. This device ensured a steady flow of water for irrigating large areas.

question: With reference to the cultural history of India, the memorizing of chronicles, dy nastic histories and epic tales was the pro fession of who of the following? (UPSC Prelims 2016)
option_1: Shramana
option_2: Parivrajaka
option_3: Agrahaarika
option_4: Magadha
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • The Magadhas were responsible for memorizing chronicles, dynastic histories, and epic tales in Indian cultural history. The Magadhas and Sutas were descendants of priestly families from the Vedic period. They were also singers and narrators who would sing praises of their patrons and tell stories of their valor.
  • The Magadhas played a significant role in India’s cultural history, and their profession was highly respected in society.
  • Shramana : means seeker, one who performs acts of austerity, ascetic.
  • Agraharika : means one who probably looked after the lands given in charity.
  • Parivrajaka : literally means ‘one who roams about all round the place.’

question:
Regarding the taxation system of Krishna Deva, the ruler of Vijayanagar, consider the following statements: (UPSC Prelims 2016)

1. The tax rate on land was fixed depending on the quality of the land.

2. Private owners of workshops paid an industries tax.

Which of the statements given above is/ are correct?
option_1: 1 only
option_2: 2 only
option_3: Both 1 and 2
option_4: Neither 1 nor 2
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • The tax rate on land was fixed depending on the quality of the land under the taxation system of Krishna Deva, the ruler of Vijayanagar.
  • The quality of land was determined by its fertility, irrigation facilities, and productivity. Land that was more fertile and had better irrigation facilities was considered higher quality and taxed at a higher rate. Land that was less fertile or lacked proper irrigation facilities was considered lower quality and taxed at a lower rate.
  • Krishna Deva Raya introduced a system of land measurement and assessment, known as Karnam or Patwari system, to determine the quality and productivity of the land. The tax was usually collected in kind, such as crops or livestock, rather than in cash.
  • During the reign of Krishna Devaraya, the rate was fixed between one third and one sixth depending on the quality of the land.
  • Private owners of workshops paid an industries tax. This tax was called Shulka and was levied by Krishna Deva Raya.
  • The tax was based on the size and type of the industry and the amount of revenue it generated. Krishna Deva Raya was the third monarch of the Tuluva dynasty and is considered oneof the greatest rulers in Indian history. He ruled the largest empire in India after the fall of the Islamic Delhi Sultanate.
  • The villages were classified into eight types for tax purpose.

They were:

1) The brahmadeya villages

2) The devadana villages

3) The mathapura villages

4) The sarvamanya villages

5) The kodagi lands

6) Rakta-kodagal lands

7) Guttagi lands

8) The rest of the villages or lands.

  • The first four types prevailed prior to the Vijayanagar times.

question: Banjaras during the medieval period of Indian history were generally............. (UPSC Prelims 2016)
option_1: Agriculturists
option_2: Warriors
option_3: Weavers
option_4: Traders
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • The Banjaras were a nomadic group of traders during the medieval period of Indian history. They transported goods like grain, salt, firewood, and cattle from one place to another. The Banjaras were also known as Lamans, Lambadi, Lambhani, Lambani, and Gormati.
  • The Banjaras played a significant role during the reign of Alauddin Khalji. Khalji used the Banjaras to transport grain to city markets. Emperor Jahangir wrote in his memoirs that the Banjaras carried grain on their bullocks from different areas and sold it in towns. The Banjaras also transported food grain for the Mughal army during military campaigns.
  • The Banjaras originated in Rajasthan and are now found all over India. The majority of the Banjara population are Hindus.

question: Which one of the following was a very important seaport in the Kakatiya king dom? (UPSC Prelims 2017)
option_1: Kakinada
option_2: Motupalli
option_3: Machilipatnam (Masulipatnam)
option_4: Nelluru
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • Motupalli was a famous and important trading seaport for the Kakatiyas. The port was a hub for the export of goods like textiles, spices, pearls, and diamonds. The kingdom also imported goods like horses, elephants, and precious metals through this port.
  • Motupalli is a village in Chinaganjam Mandal, Prakasam District in the Indian state of Andhra Pradesh. It is the site of a historic port city, one of the most ancient in India, dating back to at least the 2nd century CE.
  • The famous traveler Marco Polo also visited this port and wrote about the wealth of the Kakatiya kingdom in his travel journal.

The Kakatiya dynasty :

  • They ruled the eastern Deccan region of India from the 12th to 14th centuries. Their territory included much of present-day Telangana and Andhra Pradesh, and parts of eastern Karnataka, northern Tamil Nadu, and southern Odisha. The Kakatiyas’ capital was Orugallu, which is now known as Warangal. 
  • The Kakatiya dynasty is known for its architectural and cultural contributions. The most notable achievements are the construction of the Warangal Fort and the Thousand Pillar Temple. The rulers also played a significant role in promoting Sanskrit literature and art during their reign. 
  • The Kakatiya dynasty was founded by Betaraju, who took the name Prola Raja in the early 12th century. His successor, Rudra Deva, expanded the dynasty’s territorythrough military conquests. Ganapati Deva (1198-1262 AD) is considered the greatest ruler of the Kakatiya dynasty. He brought most of the Telugu-speaking region under the Kakatiya influence by war or diplomacy. He also turned Warangal into a center of culture, trade, and religion. 
  • In the early 14th century, Muhammad bin Tughluq, the ruler of Delhi, defeated the Kakatiya Dynasty. Following their defeat, the Kakatiya Dynasty lost their independence, and Warangal came under the direct control of the Delhi Sultanate.

 Importance of Motupalli Seaport :

  • The Motupalli seaport was a major center of trade during the Kakatiya period.
  • It was located on the east coast of India, near the mouth of the Krishna river.
  • The seaport was well-connected to other parts of the kingdom through a network of roads and waterways.
  • The port was a hub for the export of goods like textiles, spices, pearls, and diamonds.
  • The kingdom also imported goods like horses, elephants, and precious metals through this port.
  • Motupalli was an important center of maritime trade because of its strategic location.
  • It was situated close to the Bay of Bengal, which made it accessible to traders from Southeast Asia and China.
  • The port was also connected to other parts of India through the Krishna river, which facilitated inland trade.
  • The Kakatiya rulers took several measures to develop and maintain the Motupalli seaport.
  • They constructed warehouses, granaries, and other infrastructure to store goods and facilitate trade.
  • The kingdom also had a well-organized system of taxation and regulation of trade, which helped in maintaining the port’s prosperity.

question: Which one of the following foreign trav ellers elaborately discussed about dia monds and diamond mines of India? (UPSC Prelims 2018)
option_1: Francois Bernier
option_2: Jean-Baptiste Tavernier
option_3: Jean de Thevenot
option_4: Abbe Barthelemy Carre
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • Jean-Baptiste Tavernier (1605–1689) was a 17th-century French traveler and gem merchant who extensively discussed diamonds and diamond mines in India. Tavernier was a pioneer of trade between Europe and Asia, and traveled to India six times between 1630 and 1668. He is best known for discovering or purchasing the 116-carat Tavernier Blue diamond in 1666, which he later sold to Louis XIV of France.

François Bernier (1620 - 1688):

  • He was a French physician, historian, political philosopher, and traveler. He spent 12 years in India, where he served as a personal physician to Dara Shikoh, the eldest son of Emperor Shah Jahan. He also served as a scientist under Danishmand Khan.

Bernier’s observations included:

  1. A large portion of land was extremely fertile
  2. The kingdom of Bengal surpassed Egypt in agricultural production
  3. Many parts of India were sufficiently populated
  4. The land was well tilled
  • Bernier also collected important information about various fields, including succession wars, the Mughal administrative system, revenue system, and the condition of farmers.
  • He published the successful Travels in Moghul India and became part of a circle that included Racine and Boileau.

Jean de Thévenot (1633-1667) :

  • He was a French traveler, linguist, botanist, and natural scientist. He was a curious observer who traveled through Asia and wrote extensively about his experiences.
  • In 1657, Thévenot visited the Greek Islands, Egypt, and Sinai. He also made the Lent pilgrimage from Cairo to Jerusalem by caravan.
  • Thévenot was born in Paris on June 16, 1633 and attended the College of Navarre. He is the nephew of the famous French cartographer Melchisédec Thévenot (1620 1682), who is said to have influenced his interest in exploring the world.

Barthélemy Carré de Chambon (Abbé Carré) :

  • He was born in 1636 or 1640. In 1672, the French minister of finance, Jean-Baptiste Colbert, sent Carré to India to expand the French political-economic network.
  • Carré traveled from Marseille, Alexandrette, Iran, Bandar-Abbas, Bijapur, and Madras. In 1674, he left Surat for France and arrived in Marseille in 1675.

question:
With reference to Mughal India, what is/ are the difference/differences between Jagirdar and Zamindar? (UPSC Prelims 2019)

Statement 1 : Jagirdars were holders of land assignments in lieu of judicial and police duties, whereas Za mindars were holders of revenue rights without obligation to perform any duty other than revenue collection.

Statement 2 : Land as signments to Jagirdars were hereditary and revenue rights of Zamindars were not hereditary.

Select the correct answer using the code given below.
option_1: 1 only
option_2: 2 only
option_3: Both 1 and 2
option_4: Neither 1 nor 2
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • Jagirdari system was an administrative system through which the land revenue was assigned in lieu of a salary which was called the jagir.
  • The Jagirdari system did not affect the hereditary rights of the intermediaries who were collectively known as the zamindars.
  • Land assignment to Jagirdars was not hereditary and it is transferable to other jagirs also.
  • Zamindars had their own hereditary rights in land and also had the hereditary right of collecting land revenue.
  • The judicial and police duties were performed locally by zamindars and Jagirdars were holders of land assignments in lieu of military services.
  • The Mughal emperor allocated jagirs to the mansabdars.
  • The mansabdars made his own agreement for the revenue collection.

question:
Consider the following statements: (UPSC Prelims 2019)

1. In the revenue administration of Delhi Sultanate, the in-charge of revenue collection was known as ‘Amil’.

2. The lqta system of Sultans of Delhi was an ancient indigenous institution.

3. The office of ‘Mir Bakshi’ came into existence during the reign of Khalji Sultans of Delhi.

Which of the statements given above is/ are correct?
option_1: 1 only
option_2: 1 and 2 only
option_3: 3 only
option_4: 1, 2 and 3
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

The correct answer is (A) 1 Only.

  • Statement 1 is correct. The Delhi Sultanate’s revenue machinery had local officers called āmils (from Arabic āmil, meaning agent/administrator) who were responsible for collecting revenue and remitting it to the treasury. In practice amils supervised revenue collection in assigned areas, leased out revenue in some cases, and acted as the state’s revenue agents — so calling the in-charge of revenue collection an “Amil” is accurate.
  • Statement 2 is incorrect. The iqṭāʿ (iqta) system was not an indigenous ancient Indian institution but a land-assignment/ revenue-farming practice of Islamic polities that the early Muslim rulers in India adopted and adapted. The iqta system has roots in earlier Islamic/Caliphal practice (and was used by Ghaznavids, Ghurids and later Muslim rulers) and was introduced into the subcontinent under Muslim dynasties; it was therefore not an “ancient indigenous” institution of India.
  • Statement 3 is incorrect. The office of Mir Bakshi is a title that became prominent and was institutionalized under the later Muslim imperial administration — most notably the Mughals (Akbar systematized the Mir Bakhshi as the head of military affairs, recruitment, pay and intelligence). It was not an office created during the Khilji Sultans of Delhi; therefore the claim that Mir Bakshi originated under the Khiljis is wrong.

question:
Consider the following statements: (UPSC Prelims 2019)

1. Saint Nimbarka was a contemporary of Akbar.

2. Saint Kabir was greatly influenced by Shaikh Ahmad Sirhindi.

Which of the statements given above is/ are correct?
option_1: 1 only
option_2: 2 only
option_3: Both 1 and 2
option_4: Neither 1 nor 2
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • Saint Nimbarka was not a contemporary of Akbar. Saint Nimbarka lived around the 13th century, while Akbar ruled in the 16th century.
  • Saint Nimbarka was a Telugu-speaking Brahman who flourished in the 13th century in South India. He was also called Nimbaditya or Niyamananda. He was a philosopher-saint and the founder of the Nimbarka Sampradaya.
  • The Nimbarka Sampradaya is one of the four Vaishnava Sampradayas. It is also known as the Hamsa Sampradaya and the Sanakadi Sampradaya.
  • Saint Kabir (1398 or 1440-1518) was not influenced by Shaikh Ahmad Sirhindi (1564-1624/1625).
  • Sirhindi was a prominent Islamic scholar and sufi of the 16th century, while Kabir was a 15th-century poet-saint and Bhakti movement figure.
  • Kabir’s teachings were influenced by several religions, including old Brahmanic Hinduism, Hindu and Buddhist Tantrism, the teachings of Nath yogis, and the personal devotionalism from South India.
  • He was also strongly influenced by Vaishnava Saint Swami Ramananda, the Hindu bhakti leader.

question: With reference to Mian Tansen, which one of the following statements is not correct? (UPSC Prelims 2019)
option_1: Tansen was the title given to him by Emperor Akbar.
option_2: Tansen composed Dhrupads on Hindu gods and goddesses.
option_3: Tansen composed songs on his patrons.
option_4: Tansen invented many Ragas.
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • Tansen was the title given to him by Raja Vikramjit of Gwalior. Tansen was a court musician in the darbar of Raja Ramachandra of Bandavagarh (Rewa).
  • When Akbar heard of his prodigious talent, he sent a ‘firman’ to the king asking for Tansen and made him one of the Navaratnas in his court. He gave him the title of ‘Mian’. Tansen is also known as the ‘Sangit Samrat’

question: Who among the following Mughal Emperors shifted emphasis from illustrat ed manuscripts to album and individual portrait? (UPSC Prelims 2019)
option_1: Humayun
option_2: Akbar
option_3: Jahangir
option_4: Shah Jahan
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • Jahangir shifted the focus from illustrated manuscripts to chitradhara (album) and individual portraits.
  • Jahangir was a fan of art and architecture. He commissioned, preserved, and cataloged many works of art. He also had a zoo where he documented each species of animal.
  • Jahangir was exposed to European artworks through the Jesuits who visited his court. He encouraged his court painters to adopt European styles as well.
  • Jahangir employed a large studio of artists who produced paintings influenced by European portrait styles.
  • The Mughal School of painting originated in the reign of Akbar in 1560 A.D.
  • Mughal Paintings revolved around the themes of battles, hunting scenes, wildlife, portraits, etc.

Humayun (1508 - 1556) :

  • He was the second Mughal emperor of India. He ruled from 1530 to 1540 and again from 1555 until his death. His territory included what is now Pakistan, Bangladesh, Northern India, and Eastern Afghanistan.
  • Humayun was the son of Babur, the founder of the Mughal dynasty. He was known as an adventurer and was driven out of his capital, Delhi, by the Afghan usurper Shēr Shah of Sūr. Humayun was considered an ethical ruler who had a policy of not attacking any Muslims.
  • He was also known for his religious policy, which depicted him as a Sunni Muslim with an appreciation for Sufism.

During his reign, he:

1. Besieged the fortress of Kalinjar in Bundelkhand

2. Defeated the Afghans at Douhrua

3. Drove out Sultan Mahmood Lodhi from Jaunpur

4. Defeated Bahadur Shah of Gujarat

  • Humayun died in Delhi on January 27, 1556 after tripping and fracturing his head while carrying books. He was known as “the luckless one”.

Akbar (1556-1605) :

  • Emperor Akbar founded the city in the name of “ILLAHABAS” which later became ALLAHABAD.
  • He introduced the new religion as Din Ilahi.
  • He introduced the Mansabdari system.
  • Akbar defeated Hemu in the second battle of Panipat.
  • He built Ibadat Khana in Fatehpur Sikri.
  • He followed the policy of Sulh-i-kul.

Jahangir (1605-1627) :

  • Jahangir was the eldest son of Akbar.
  • Jahangir executed the fifth Sikh Guru, Arjun Dev.
  • Under Jahangir, Mughal paintings reached its zenith.

Shah Jahan (1627-1658) :

  • He was the son of Jahangir.
  • A French traveller, Tavernier visited during his reign.
  • Diwan-i-Khas and Diwan-i-Am, a hall of private and public audiences are built by Shah Jahan.

question: With reference to medieval India, which one of the following is the correct se quence in ascending order in terms of size? (UPSC Prelims 2021)
option_1: Paragana- Sarkar- Suba
option_2: Sarkar-Paragana-Suba
option_3: Suba-Sarkar-Paragana
option_4: Paragana-Suba-Sarkar
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  •  In the 16th century, the Mughal emperor Akbar organized the empire into three administrative levels:

1. Subah: Roughly equivalent to a state or province.

2. Sarkar: Roughly equivalent to a district.

3. Pargana: Roughly equivalent to a district subdivision.

  • The Mughal empire was initially divided into 12 subahs, which were later expanded to 15 during Akbar’s reign. •
  • The correct sequence in ascending order of size for medieval India is: Pargana, Sarkar, Suba.

A Sarkar also included:

1. A Shiqdar (military officer)

2. An Amin or Munsif (Civilian Judge)

3. One Fotahdar (treasurer)

4. Two Karkuns (clerks or writers)

question:
With reference to Indian history, which of the following statements is/are correct? (UPSC Prelims 2021)

1. The Nizamat of Arcot emerged out of Hyderabad State.

2. The Mysore Kingdom emerged out of Vijaynagara Empire.

3. Rohilkhand Kingdom was formed out of the territories occupied by Ahmad Shah Durrani.

Select the correct answer using the codes given below :
option_1: 1 and 2
option_2: 2 Only
option_3: 2 and 3
option_4: 3 Only
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • The Nizamat of Arcot did not emerge from Hyderabad State. It was a political office in the Carnatic region, which was under the control of the Nawab of Arcot. The Nizamat of Arcot was established in the Carnatic region under the Mughals, and it was distinct from Hyderabad.
  • The Nizam of Arcot emerged as an independent state after the decline of the Mughal Empire in the first half of the eighteenth century. It was a protected area for the Hyderabad state, but it did not get out of it. The
  • Nizamat of Arcot was a British administrative division in south India, which was created in 1768. Arcot is a town and urban area of Ranipet district in the state of Tamil Nadu, India.
  • The Mysore Kingdom emerged from the Vijayanagara Empire in the early 18th century. The Wodeyar dynasty ruled the Mysore Kingdom, which played a significant role in South India’s history.
  • The Vijayanagara Empire was established in 1336 after rebellions against Tughluq rule in the Deccan. In 1565, the Deccan sultanates united and defeated the Vijayanagara Empire in the Battle of Talikota.
  • In 1704, Krishnaraja Wodeyar I declared independence from the Vijayanagara Empire and became the founder of the Mysore Kingdom. In 1761, Hyder Ali overthrew the regaining dynasty in Mysore and established his own control. In 1782, Tipu Sultan succeeded Hyder Ali.
  • The Rohilkhand Kingdom was not formed out of territories occupied by Ahmad Shah Durrani. The Rohilkhand Kingdom was established in the mid-18th century by Hafiz Rahmat Khan, a Rohilla Afghan. The Rohillas were Afghan settlers who established their dominance in the Rohilkhand region of North India before the invasions of Ahmad Shah Durrani.
  • The Rohilkhand region is located in present-day Uttar Pradesh and includes the cities of Amroha, Bareilly, Bijnore, Budaun, Khutar, Moradabad, Najibabad, Pilibhit, Rampur, Bahjoi, Kakrala, and Shahjahanpur. The kingdom covers an area of about 10,000 square miles, or 25,000 square kilometers, around the city of Bareilly.

question:
According to Portuguese writer Nuniz, the women in Vijayanagara Empire were expert in which of the following areas? (UPSC Prelims 2021)

1. Wrestling

2. Astrology

3. Accounting

4. Soothsaying

Select the correct answer using the codes given below.
option_1: 1, 2 and 3 Only
option_2: 1, 3 and 4 Only
option_3: 2 and 4 Only
option_4: 1, 2, 3 and 4
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • According to Portuguese writer Fernao Nuniz, women in the Vijayanagara Empire were experts in wrestling, astrology, accounting, and soothsaying. Nuniz visited the empire between 1535 and 1537 AD.
  •  Wrestling : Women in the Vijayanagara Empire were trained in wrestling and were known to participate in the sport.
  •  Astrology : Women were also skilled in astrology and were consulted for horoscopes and predictions.
  •  Accounting : Women in the empire were also trained in accounting and were responsible for managing the household finances.
  •  Soothsaying : Women were also skilled in soothsaying or predicting the future based on omens and signs.
  •  Nuniz also wrote that women in the Vijayanagara Empire were employed in other roles, including:
  • Dancers, Domestic servants, Palanquin bearers, Judges, Bailiffs, Watch women.
  • Nuniz also wrote that women in the Vijayanagara Empire were trained in wrestling and were known to participate in the sport.

question:
Consider the following statements: (UPSC Prelims 2021)

1. It was during the reign of Iltutmish that Chengiz Khan reached the Indus in pursuit of the fugitive Khawarezm prince.

2. It was during the reign of Muhammad bin Tughluq that Taimur occupied Multan and crossed the Indus.

3. It was during the reign of Deva Raya II of Vijayanagara Empire that Vasco da Gama reached the coast of Kerala.

Which of the statements given above is/ are correct?
option_1: 1 Only
option_2: 1 and 2
option_3: 3 Only
option_4: 2 and 3
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • Chengiz Khan reached the Indus River in pursuit of the fugitive Khawarezm prince during the reign of Iltutmish.
  • Chengiz Khan was the Mongol ruler who attacked the Khwarezmian Empire, which was located in present-day Iran and Central Asia. The Khwarezm prince, Jalal ad-Din Mingburnu, sought refuge in India, and Iltutmish allowed him to settle in India.
  • Iltutmish was the Sultan of Delhi from 1211 to 1236. His reign is considered significant because it marked the rise of the first independent Turkish ruler in India.
  • The statement that Taimur occupied Multan and crossed the Indus during the reign of Muhammad bin Tughluq is incorrect.
  • In 1398, Timur invaded India during the reign of Nasir-ud-Din Mahmud Shah Tughluq, the last ruler of the Tughlaq dynasty. On September 30, 1398, Timur crossed the Indus River and captured Multan in October.
  • Timur was a great patron of art and architecture, and his reign introduced the Timurid Renaissance. He reigned over a vast empire that included modern-day Turkey, Syria, Iraq, Kuwait, and Iran, as well as parts of Kazakhstan, Afghanistan, Russia, Turkmenistan, Uzbekistan, Kyrgyzstan, Pakistan, and India.
  • Vasco da Gama reached the coast of Kerala in 1498, which was more than 50 years after the death of Deva Raya II. Deva Raya II ruled the Vijayanagara Empire from 1424 to 1446.
  • Vasco da Gama was a Portuguese navigator who opened up the sea route from western Europe to the East in the late 15th and early 16th centuries. In 1498, he landed with 170 men at Kappad beach, near Kozhikode, Kerala.

question:
Consider the following statements: (UPSC Prelims 2021)

1. St. Francis Xavier was one of the founding members of the Jesuit Order.

2. St. Francis Xavier died in Goa and a church is dedicated to him there.

3. The Feast of St. Francis Xavier is celebrated in Goa each year.

Which of the statements given above are correct?
option_1: 1 and 2 Only
option_2: 2 and 3 Only
option_3: 1 and 3 Only
option_4: 1, 2 and 3
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • St. Francis Xavier was one of the first seven members of the Jesuit order. He was also a co-founder of the Society of Jesus.
  • Xavier was a Spanish Jesuit missionary in the 1500s. He was born in 1506 in the Basque region of northern Spain. He is known for his missionary work in India, Southeast Asia, and Japan. He is also the patron saint of Roman Catholic missions.
  • Xavier was known for his enthusiasm, passion, and charisma. He was also known for being decisive, cheerful, and practical. Xavier is honored by the Catholic Church, other Christian churches, and the Jesuit order.
  • He arrived in Goa, India, on May 6, 1542. Over the next 12 years, Francis led a series of overseas missions, mainly in the Portuguese Empire of the time - Mozambique, Malacca and notably Goa.
  • He was the first Christian missionary to travel Japan and on to Borneo and the Moluccan Islands.
  • On December 3, 1552, Francis died of fever on the island of Sancian (Shangchuan, off the Chinese coast).
  • The Feast of St. Francis Xavier is celebrated in Goa on December 3rd each year. It marks the death anniversary of the saint and is also known as the Goinchea Saibache Fest, or “Lord of Goa Festival”.
  • The feast day commemorates St. Francis Xavier’s faith and missionary work in Europe and Asia. St. Francis Xavier is the patron saint of Goa, and festivities in Goa may begin as early as the end of November.

question: In medieval India, the term “Fanam” re ferred to..............  (UPSC Prelims 2022)
option_1: Clothing
option_2: Coins
option_3: Ornaments
option_4: Weapons
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • In medieval India, the term “Fanam” referred to coins. Fanam is the anglicized form of the native word panam, which means “coin” or “wealth”.
  • Fanam was a gold currency used in Southern India from the 17th to 19th centuries.
  • The Madras Presidency issued the fanam until 1815, and it circulated alongside the Indian rupee.
  • The fanam was a small silver coin, subdivided into 80 copper cash. The gold pagoda was worth 42 fanams, and the rupee was worth 12 fanams.
  • The words “Fanam” and “Panam” literally mean “money” and are still used as a synonym for “wealth” in Kerala in the native language of Malayalam.

question:
With reference to Indian history, consid er the following statements: (UPSC Prelims 2022)

1.T he first Mongol invasion of India happened during the reign of Jalal ud-din Khalji.

2. During the reign of Ala-ud-din Khalji, one Mongol assault marched up to Delhi and besieged the city.

3. Muhammad - bin - Tughlaq temporarily lost portions of north west of his kingdom to Mongols.

Which of the statements given above is/ are correct?
option_1: 1 and 2
option_2: 2 only
option_3: 1 and 3
option_4: 3 only
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • The first Mongol invasion of India happened in 1221, during the reign of Sultan Iltutmish, not Jalal-ud-din Khalji.
  • The Mongol Empire invaded the Indian subcontinent several times from 1221 to 1327.
  • In 1297, during the reign of Ala-ud-din Khalji, a Mongol assault led by Ghiyas ud-din Tughlaq marched up to Delhi and besieged the city.
  • The Mongols plundered the area around Delhi and besieged the fort for two months, but they were unable to capture it and withdrew.
  • The Mongols attacked Delhi twice during the reign of Alauddin Khalji, the Delhi Sultanate ruler. The first attack was in 1299 and the second was in 1302-03.
  • In 1299, the Mongols invaded India, led by Qutlugh Khwaja. The Mongols encamped at Kili, near Delhi, and the Delhi Sultan led an army to stop their advance. The Battle of Kili was fought between the Mongols and the Delhi Sultanate.
  • In 1303, the Mongols launched another invasion of the Delhi Sultanate.
  • The Delhi Sultan was away at Chittor when the Mongols started their march, but he returned to Delhi in a hurry. In 1311, Alauddin Khalji ordered a massacre of the “New Muslims” after some Mongol amirs of Delhi conspired to kill him.
  • According to chronicler Ziauddin Barani, 20,000 or 30,000 Mongols were killed as a result of this order.
  • Muhammad-bin-Tughlaq did not lose any portion of his kingdom to the Mongols, despite facing Mongol invasions.
  • Muhammad-bin-Tughlaq was the 18th Sultan of Delhi, reigning from 1325 until his death in 1351. The Tughlaqs were the longest-ruling dynasty of the Delhi Sultanates.

question: With reference to Indian history, who of the following were known as “Kulah-Da ran” ? (UPSC Prelims 2022)
option_1: Arab merchants
option_2: Qalandars
option_3: Persian calligraphists
option_4: Sayyids
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • In Indian history, the term “Kulah Daran” refers to the Sayyids, a group of people who were Persian immigrants to India during the medieval period.
  • The term translates to “cap-wearers” and comes from the conical cap or kulah worn by them as a mark of distinction.
  • The Sayyids were a theological class during the Delhi Sultanate era and claimed descent from the Prophet Muhammad.
  • They commanded special respect in Muslim society, and even Timur protected the life of Sayyids during his invasion in India.

question: “Yogavãsistha” was translated into Per sian by Nizamuddin Panipati during the reign of.............. (UPSC Prelims 2022)
option_1: Akbar
option_2: Humayun
option_3: Shahjahan
option_4: Aurangzeb
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • Nizamuddin Panipati translated Yogavasistha into Persian during the reignof Akbar.
  • The translation project likely began around 1575–1576, when Shaikh Bhawan, a Brahman convert, joined Akbar’s court.
  • Panipati translated the text as an appendix to the Ramayana and dedicated it to Prince Salim.
  • Yogavasistha, also known as Maha Ramayana, is a Hindu philosophical text that dates back to the 11th–14th century.
  • It is considered one of the longest Sanskrit texts, after the Mahabharata, and is an important text on Yoga.
  • The text is associated with Advaita Vedanta philosophy and discusses illusion, non duality, yoga, and liberation.
  • The text was translated into Persian multiple times during the Mughal rule, on orders of Akbar, Jahangir, and Dara Shikoh.

question: The world’s second tallest statue in sitting pose of Ramanuja was inaugurated by the Prime Minister of India at Hyderabad re cently. Which one of the following state ments correctly represents the teachings of Ramanuja? (UPSC Prelims 2022)
option_1: The best means of salvation was devotion.
option_2: Vedas are eternal, self-existent and wholly authoritative.
option_3: Logical arguments were meant for the highest bliss.
option_4: Salvation was to be obtained through meditation.
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • According to Ramanuja, the best way to achieve salvation is through devotion to God, or bhakti. He believed that devotion was a disciplined practice that involved surrendering to God and serving Him. Ramanuja also advocated for surrender to God (prapatti) as a means to achieve moksha, or liberation from the cycle of birth and death.
  • Ramanuja was born in Tamil Nadu in the 11th century and was influenced by the Alvars.
  • He firmly believed that intense devotion to Vishnu was the best means to attainsalvation.
  • He propounded the doctrine of Vishishtadvaita.
  • The outdoor 216-ft Statue of Equality will be the world’s second tallest statue featuring a sitting posture.
  • It is composed of ‘panchaloha’, a combination of five metals comprising gold, silver, copper, brass, and zinc.
  • Born in 1017 in Sri Perumbudur, Tamil Nadu, Sri Ramanujacharya liberated millions from social, cultural, gender, educational and economic discrimination with the foundational conviction that every human is equal regardless of nationality, gender, race, caste, or creed.

question:
Consider the following dynasties......... (UPSC Prelims 2023)

1. Hoysala

2. Gahadavala

3. Kakatiya

4. Yadava

How many of the above dynasties established their kingdoms in early Eighth century AD?
option_1: Only one
option_2: Only two
option_3: Only three
option_4: None
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

The Hoysala Dynasty :

  • It was a Kannadiga power that ruled most of what is now Karnataka between the 10th and 14th centuries. The dynasty is known for its temple architecture, including the Chennakesava Temple, Hoysaleswara Temple, and Kesava Temple.
  • The Hoysalas were initially subordinate to the Western Chalukyas until 1187, when Veera Ballala II freed them. The Hoysala Empire is remembered for its temple architecture.
  • The Hoysala Dynasty was founded by Sala, who was succeeded by Vinayaditya and Balalla I. The greatest ruler of the Hoysala dynasty was Vishnuvardhana (Bittideva). He defeated the Cholas in the battle of Talakadu, earning him the title Talkadugonda.
  • The Hoysala Empire King Ballal III resisted the Muslim invasion for nearly twenty years but was eventually killed in 1343 CE by Delhi Sultanate forces at the Battle of Madurai.

The Gahadavala dynasty :

  • It was a Rajput dynasty that ruled parts of present-day Uttar Pradesh and Bihar from the 11th to 12th centuries. The dynasty’s capital was located in Banaras, and for a short time, they also controlled Kannauj. The dynasty was established by King Yashovigraha. The first Gahadavala king was Chandradeva, who ruled from 1089 1103.
  • The dynasty’s dominance peaked under the reign of Govindachandra. The dynasty was overthrown by the invasions of Muhammad of Ghur. The Gahadavalas were succeeded by the Delhi Sultanate in the 12th century, when their last monarch Harishchandra was defeated in battle.

The Kakatiya dynasty :

  • It was a South Indian dynasty that ruled from the 12th to the 14th century. Their territory included most of present-day Telangana and Andhra Pradesh, as well as parts of eastern Karnataka, northern Tamil Nadu, and southern Odisha.
  • The Kakatiyas were known for their patronage of art, literature, and architecture, particularly in the form of temple construction. Their architecture is a fusion of Dravidian architecture and Nagara Bhumija styles.
  • The name “Kakatiya” comes from the goddess Kakati, a mother goddess Durga, who was venerated by the dynasty. The Kakatiyas survived until the Turks’ attack in the 14th century.

The Yadava dynasty :

  • It is also known as the Seuna dynasty, was a Hindu dynasty that ruled the Deccanregion of South India from the 11th to the 14th centuries CE. The dynasty’s history dates back to the ninth century.
  • The Yadava dynasty ruled for about 300 years as feudatories of the Rashtrakutas and the Chalukyas. The dynasty became paramount in the Deccan under Bhillama (c. 1187–91), who founded Devagiri (later Daulatabad) as his capital.
  • At its peak, the Yadava dynasty ruled a realm stretching from the Narmada river in the north to the Tungabhadra river in the south.

question: Who among the following rulers of Vi jayanagara Empire constructed a Large dam across Tungabhadra River and a canal-cum-aqueduct several kilometres long from the river to the Capital city? (UPSC Prelims 2023)
option_1: Devaraya I
option_2: Mallikarjuna
option_3: Vira Vijaya
option_4: Virupaksha
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • Devaraya I, also known as Devaraya II or Devaraja II, was the ruler of the Vijayanagara Empire who constructed a large dam across the Tungabhadra River and a canal-cum-aqueduct from the river to the capital city.
  • Devaraya I ruled from 1406 CE to 1422 CE and is known for several significant contributions to infrastructure, including water management systems. He was a warrior, statesman, and builder.
  • The dam and canal-cum-aqueduct were great feats of engineering that provided water for irrigation and drinking water for the people of the capital city.

question: Who among the following rulers of Me dieval Gujarat surrendered Diu to Portuguese? (UPSC Prelims 2023)
option_1: Ahmad Shah
option_2: Mahmud Begarha
option_3: Bahadur Shah
option_4: Muhammad Shah
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • Bahadur Shah, the ruler of medieval Gujarat, surrendered Diu to the Portuguese in 1534.
  • The surrender was part of an agreement with the Portuguese in exchange for their assistance against the Mughals and protection for the realm.
  • The surrender of Diu was a significant blow to the Gujarat Sultanate and marked the beginning of its decline. It also paved the way for the Portuguese to establish their dominance in the Indian Ocean.
  • Diu remained under Portuguese control for over four centuries, until it was finally annexed by India in 1961.

question: Who of the following rulers of medieval India gave permission to the Portuguese to build a fort at Bhatkal ? (UPSC Prelims 2024)
option_1: Krishnadevaraya
option_2: Narasimha Saluva
option_3: Muhammad Shah III
option_4: Yusuf Adil Shah
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • Bhatkal is port in Uttara Kannada district in Karnataka. It is located on river Sharabi.
  • Bhatkal came into focus when Portuguese arrived in India. From that time Bhatkal chiefs were paying tributes to the Portuguese rulers.
  • Emperor Krishnadevaraya allowed the Portuguese to build a fort here in 1510. Hence, option (a) is correct.

question: The irrigation device called 'Araghatta' was: (UPSC Prelims 2025)
option_1: a water bag made of leather pulled over a pulley
option_2: a large wheel with earthen pots tied to the outer ends of its spokes
option_3: a larger earthen pot driven by bullocks
option_4: a large water bucket pulled up by rope directly by hand
solution_text:
Explanation: (b) a large wheel with earthen pots tied to the outer ends of its spokes

  • Araghatta was a traditional Indian water-lifting device. It involved a large wheel with pots attached to its rim, which, when rotated (usually by bullocks), lifted water from a well or river for irrigation.
